Theory

Definition: Stochastic Process

A stochastic process is a collection of random variables, {Xt,t∈I}\{X_t, t \in \mathcal{I} \}{Xt​,t∈I}. where I\mathcal{I}I is the index set of the process and S\mathcal{S}S is the common state space of the random variables XtX_tXt​.

Definition: Markov Property

A process fulfills the Markov property if, for any t0∈It_0 \in \mathcal{I}t0​∈I, whenever Xt0X_{t_0}Xt0​​ is known, XtX_tXt​ (with t>t0t > t_0t>t0​) is independent of the values for XsX_sXs​ for all s<t0s < t_0s<t0​.

Recall: Conditional Probability and Independence

Given the events AAA and BBB, the conditional probability of AAA given BBB is defined as,

P(A∣B)=P(A∩B)P(B)P(A \mid B) = \frac{P(A \cap B)}{P(B)}P(A∣B)=P(B)P(A∩B)​

The events AAA and BBB are independent if P(A∩B)=P(A)P(B)P(A \cap B) = P(A) P(B)P(A∩B)=P(A)P(B), or equivalently, if P(A∣B)=P(A)P(A \mid B) = P(A)P(A∣B)=P(A).

The law of total probability states that if B1,B2,…,BnB_1, B_2, \ldots, B_nB1​,B2​,…,Bn​ is a sequence of events that partitions S. Then,

P(A)=∑i=1nP(A∩Bi)=∑i=1nP(A∣Bi)P(Bi)P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \cap B_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(A \mid B_i) P(B_i)P(A)=i=1∑n​P(A∩Bi​)=i=1∑n​P(A∣Bi​)P(Bi​)

Thus, Bayes’ law follow directly from the definition of conditional probability and the law of total probability,

P(B∣A)=P(A∣B)P(B)P(A)P(B \mid A) = \frac{P(A \mid B) P(B)}{P(A)}P(B∣A)=P(A)P(A∣B)P(B)​
Definition: Law of Total Expectation
E[Y]=E[E[Y∣X]]\mathbb{E}[Y] = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X]]E[Y]=E[E[Y∣X]]
Definition: Law of Total Variance

Recall that, by definition,

Var(Y)≔E[(Y−E[Y])2]=E[Y2]−(E[Y])2\mathrm{Var}(Y) \coloneqq \mathbb{E}\left[(Y - \mathbb{E}[Y])^2\right] = \mathbb{E}[Y^2] - (\mathbb{E}[Y])^2Var(Y):=E[(Y−E[Y])2]=E[Y2]−(E[Y])2

Similarly, we have for the conditional variance,

Var(Y∣X)≔EY∣X=x[(Y−E[Y∣X])2∣X]\mathrm{Var}(Y \mid X) \coloneqq \mathbb{E}_{Y \mid X = x}\left[(Y - \mathbb{E}[Y \mid X])^2 \mid X\right]Var(Y∣X):=EY∣X=x​[(Y−E[Y∣X])2∣X]

With these definitions, we can state the law of total variance as,

Var(Y)=E[Var(Y∣X)]+Var(E[Y∣X])\mathrm{Var}(Y) = \mathbb{E}[\mathrm{Var}(Y \mid X)] + \mathrm{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Y \mid X])Var(Y)=E[Var(Y∣X)]+Var(E[Y∣X])
Definition: Markov Chain

Let SSS be a discrete set (not necessarily finite), called the state space. A Markov Chain is a sequence of random variables X0,X1,X2,…X_0, X_1, X_2, \ldotsX0​,X1​,X2​,… taking values in SSS, with the property,

π(Xn+1∣X0,X1,…,Xn)=π(Xn+1∣Xn)\pi(X_{n+1} \mid X_0, X_1, \ldots, X_n) = \pi(X_{n+1} \mid X_n)π(Xn+1​∣X0​,X1​,…,Xn​)=π(Xn+1​∣Xn​)

for all n≥1n \geq 1n≥1.

The chain is said to be time-homogeneous if, for all n≥0n \geq 0n≥0 1We generally assume this unless otherwise stated.,

π(Xn+1∣Xn)=π(X1∣X0)\pi(X_{n+1} \mid X_n) = \pi(X_1 \mid X_0)π(Xn+1​∣Xn​)=π(X1​∣X0​)

The transition matrix is defined with,

Pij=π(Xn+1=j∣Xn=i)P_{ij} = \pi(X_{n+1} = j \mid X_n = i)Pij​=π(Xn+1​=j∣Xn​=i)

Further, a stochastic matrix is a square matrix PPP with non-negative entries, satisfying P1=1P \mathbf{1} = \mathbf{1}P1=1, where 1\mathbf{1}1 is a row vector of ones (i.e., all rows sum to one).

Lastly, all transition matrices are stochastic matrices, and all stochastic matrices can be used as transition matrices.

Definition: Limiting Distribution

A limiting distribution for a Markov chain with transition matrix PPP is a probability vector vvv such that,

lim⁡n→∞(Pn)ij=vj\lim_{n \to \infty} (P^n)_{ij} = v_jn→∞lim​(Pn)ij​=vj​

for all states iii and jjj.

An equivalent formulation is, the limit lim⁡n→∞(Pn)ij\lim_{n \to \infty} (P^n)_{ij}limn→∞​(Pn)ij​ exists and does not depend on iii.

Another equivalent formulation is, lim⁡n→∞Pn\lim_{n \to \infty} P^nlimn→∞​Pn is a stochastic matrix with all rows identical.

Further, a Markov chain has either no or one unique limiting distribution. If a limiting distribution exists, its probabilities correspond to the proportion of time steps the chain spends at each state in the long run.

Definition: Stationary Distribution

A stationary distribution for a Markov chain is a distribution that is unchanged when applying one step of the Markov chain. If PPP is the transition matrix, then a probability vector vvv represents a stationary distribution if and only if,

vP=vvP = vvP=v

A Markov chain can have zero, one, or many stationary distributions. Limiting distributions are always stationary distributions (but not necessarily vice versa).

Definition: Regular Transition Matrices

A stochastic matrix PPP is positive if all entries are strictly positive, i.e., Pij>0P_{ij} > 0Pij​>0 for all iii and jjj. A stochastic matrix PPP is regular if PnP^nPn is positive for some n>0n > 0n>0.

Theorem: Limit Theorem for Regular Markov Chains

If the transition matrix PPP is regular, the limiting distribution exists. There are no other stationary distributions and the limiting distribution is positive, i.e., all states have positive probability in the limiting distribution.

Theorem: Random Walks on Undirected Graphs

Random walks in a Markov chains (i.e., undircted graphs) have the stationary distribution vvv with,

vi=deg(i)Sv_i = \frac{\mathrm{deg}(i)}{S}vi​=Sdeg(i)​

where deg(i)\mathrm{deg}(i)deg(i) is the degree of node iii (i.e., the number of edges going into node iii) and SSS is the sum over all nodes of their degrees. Further, weighted random walks in a Markov chains (i.e., weighted undircted graphs) have the stationary distribution vvv with,

vi=w(i)ev_i = \frac{w(i)}{e}vi​=ew(i)​

where w(i)w(i)w(i) is the sum of the weights of the edges going into node iii, and eee is the total sum over all nodes of their w(i)w(i)w(i) values.

Intuition: Moving Between States

State jjj is accessible from state iii if (Pn)ij>0(P^n)_{ij} > 0(Pn)ij​>0 for some n≥0n \geq 0n≥0. States iii and jjj communicate if iii is accessible from jjj and jjj is accessible from iii.

“Communication” is transitive, i.e., if iii communicates with jjj and jjj communicates with kkk, then iii communicates with kkk.

Communication is an equivalence relation, subdividing all states into communication classes. Communication classes can be found for example by drawing transition graphs.

Lastly, a Markov chain is irreducible if it has exactly one communication class.

Definition: Recurrence and Transience

Let TjT_jTj​ be the first passage time to state jjj,

Tj=min⁡{n>0:Xn=j}T_j = \min \{n > 0 : X_n = j \}Tj​=min{n>0:Xn​=j}

Let fjf_jfj​ be the probability that a chain starting at jjj will return to jjj,

fj=P(Tj<∞∣X0=j)f_j = P(T_j < \infty \mid X_0 = j)fj​=P(Tj​<∞∣X0​=j)

A state jjj is recurrent if a chain starting at jjj will eventually revisit jjj, i.e., if fj=1f_j = 1fj​=1.

A state jjj is transient if a chain starting at jjj has a positive probability of never revisiting jjj, i.e., if fj<1f_j < 1fj​<1.

Note

The expected number of visits at jjj when the chain starts at iii is given by ∑n=0∞(Pn)ij\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} (P^n)_{ij}∑n=0∞​(Pn)ij​.

jjj is recurrent if and only if ∑n=0∞(Pn)jj=∞\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} (P^n)_{jj} = \infty∑n=0∞​(Pn)jj​=∞.

jjj is transient if and only if ∑n=0∞(Pn)jj<∞\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} (P^n)_{jj} < \infty∑n=0∞​(Pn)jj​<∞.

Note: Communication Classes

The states of a communication class are either all recurrent or all transient. The states of a finite irreducible Markov chain are all recurrent.

If a state is recurrent, only states inside its communication class are accessible from it. If no states outside a finite communication class are accessible from it, then the class consists of recurrent states.

Theorem: Limit Theorem for Finite Irreducible Markov Chains
Recall

In a finite irreducible Markov chain, all states are recurrent.

Let μj=E[Tj∣X0=j]\mu_j = \mathbb{E}[T_j \mid X_0 = j]μj​=E[Tj​∣X0​=j] be the expected return time to state jjj. Then μj<∞\mu_j < \inftyμj​<∞ for all states jjj and the vector vvv with vj=1μjv_j = \frac{1}{\mu_j}vj​=μj​1​ is the unique stationary distribution.

Further,

vj=lim⁡n→∞1n∑m=0n−1(Pm)ijv_j = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{m = 0}^{n - 1} (P^m)_{ij}vj​=n→∞lim​n1​m=0∑n−1​(Pm)ij​
Note

All finite regular Markov chains are finite irreducible Markov chains, but not vice versa. The overall conclusion here is weaker than that for finite regular Markov chains. Not all finite irreducible Markov chains have limiting distributions.

Definition: Periodicity

The period of a state iii is the greatest common divisor of all n>0n > 0n>0 such that (Pn)ii>0(P^n)_{ii} > 0(Pn)ii​>0. All states of a communication class have the same period.

A Markov chain is periodic if it is irreducible and all states have period greater than 1.

A Markov chain is aperiodic if it is irreducible and all states have period 1.

Definition: Ergodic Markov Chains

A Markov chain is ergodic if,

  1. It is irreducible.

  2. It is aperiodic.

  3. All states are positive recurrent (i..e, have finite expected return times (which always is true if the state space is finite)).

Theorem: Fundamental Limit Theorem for Ergodic Markov Chains

There exists a unique stationary distribution vvv which is the limiting distribution of the chain.

Note

We can also show that a finite Markov chain is ergodic if and only if its transition matrix is regular.

Definition: Time Reversibility

Let PPP be the transition matrix of an irreducible Markov chain with stationary distribution vvv. The chain is time reversible if, when running from its stationary distribution, it looks the same moving forward as backwards, i.e.,

π(Xk=i,Xk+1=j)=π(Xk=j,Xk+1=i)\pi(X_k = i, X_{k + 1} = j) = \pi(X_k = j, X_{k + 1} = i)π(Xk​=i,Xk+1​=j)=π(Xk​=j,Xk+1​=i)

This may also be written as viPij=vjPjiv_i P_{ij} = v_j P_{ji}vi​Pij​=vj​Pji​ for all states iii and jjj. It is called the detailed balance equations.

Note

If xxx is a probability vector satisfying xiPij=xjPjix_i P_{ij} = x_j P_{ji}xi​Pij​=xj​Pji​ for all states iii and jjj, then xxx is the stationary distribution of the chain, and the chain is time reversible.

Proof

We have,

(xP)j=∑ixiPij=∑ixjPji=xj∑iPji=xj\begin{align*} (xP)_j & = \sum_{i} x_i P_{ij} \newline & = \sum_i x_j P_{ji} \newline & = x_j \sum_i P_{ji} \newline & = x_j \end{align*}(xP)j​​=i∑​xi​Pij​=i∑​xj​Pji​=xj​i∑​Pji​=xj​​

Thus, xP=xxP = xxP=x, so xxx is the stationary distribution.

If a Markov chain is defined as a random walk on a weighted undirected graph, then it is time reversible.

Proof

Let w(i,j)w(i, j)w(i,j) be the weight of the edge between nodes iii and jjj (0 if no edge exists). Let w(i)=∑kw(i,k)w(i) = \sum_{k} w(i, k)w(i)=∑k​w(i,k) be the sum of weights of edges going into node iii. The transition matrix is given by Pij=w(i,j)w(i)P_{ij} = \frac{w(i, j)}{w(i)}Pij​=w(i)w(i,j)​. Let vi=w(i)ev_i = \frac{w(i)}{e}vi​=ew(i)​, where eee is the total sum over all nodes of their w(i)w(i)w(i) values. Then,

viPij=w(i)e⋅w(i,j)w(i)=w(i,j)e=w(j,i)e=w(j)e⋅w(j,i)w(j)=vjPji\begin{align*} v_i P_{ij} & = \frac{w(i)}{e} \cdot \frac{w(i, j)}{w(i)} \newline & = \frac{w(i, j)}{e} \newline & = \frac{w(j, i)}{e} \newline & = \frac{w(j)}{e} \cdot \frac{w(j, i)}{w(j)} \newline & = v_j P_{ji} \end{align*}vi​Pij​​=ew(i)​⋅w(i)w(i,j)​=ew(i,j)​=ew(j,i)​=ew(j)​⋅w(j)w(j,i)​=vj​Pji​​

Thus, the detailed balance equations are satisfied, so the chain is time reversible.

If a finite Markov chain is time reversible, it can be represented as a random walk on a weighted undirected graph.

Proof

Let PPP be the transition matrix of a finite time reversible Markov chain with stationary distribution vvv. Let w(i,j)=viPijw(i, j) = v_i P_{ij}w(i,j)=vi​Pij​ be the weight of the edge between nodes iii and jjj. Then,

∑jw(i,j)=∑jviPij=vi∑jPij=vi\sum_{j} w(i, j) = \sum_{j} v_i P_{ij} = v_i \sum_{j} P_{ij} = v_ij∑​w(i,j)=j∑​vi​Pij​=vi​j∑​Pij​=vi​

Thus, the sum of weights of edges going into node iii is viv_ivi​. The transition matrix is given by,

Pij=w(i,j)∑kw(i,k)=viPijvi=PijP_{ij} = \frac{w(i, j)}{\sum_{k} w(i, k)} = \frac{v_i P_{ij}}{v_i} = P_{ij}Pij​=∑k​w(i,k)w(i,j)​=vi​vi​Pij​​=Pij​

Thus, the Markov chain can be represented as a random walk on a weighted undirected graph.

Definition: Absorbing Chains

State iii is absorbing if Pii=1P_{ii} = 1Pii​=1a A Markov chain is an absorbing chain if it has at least one absorbing state.

By reordering the states, the transition matrix for an absorbing chain can be written in the block form,

P=[QR0I]P = \begin{bmatrix} Q & R \newline 0 & I \end{bmatrix}P=[Q0​RI​]

where III is the identity matrix, 000 is a matrix of zeroes, and QQQ corresponds to transient states. We can prove by induction that,

Pn=[Qn(I+Q+Q2+…+Qn−1)R0I]P^n = \begin{bmatrix} Q^n & (I + Q + Q^2 + \ldots + Q^{n - 1}) R \newline 0 & I \end{bmatrix}Pn=[Qn0​(I+Q+Q2+…+Qn−1)RI​]

Taking the limit and using lim⁡n→∞Qn=0\lim_{n \to \infty} Q^n = 0limn→∞​Qn=0 (since all states corresponding to QQQ are transient), we have,

lim⁡n→∞Pn=[0(I−Q)−1R0I]=[0FR0I]\lim_{n \to \infty} P^n = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & (I - Q)^{-1} R \newline 0 & I \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & FR \newline 0 & I \end{bmatrix}n→∞lim​Pn=[00​(I−Q)−1RI​]=[00​FRI​]

where F=(I−Q)−1=lim⁡n→∞(I+Q+Q2+…+Qn−1)F = (I - Q)^{-1} = \lim_{n \to \infty} (I + Q + Q^2 + \ldots + Q^{n - 1})F=(I−Q)−1=limn→∞​(I+Q+Q2+…+Qn−1) is called the fundamental matrix.

The probability to be absorbed in a particular absorbing state given a start in a transient state is given by the entries of FRFRFR.

Further, the expected number of visits in state jjj for a chain that starts in the transient state iii is given by FijF_{ij}Fij​ (as this is equal to ∑n=0∞(Pn)ij\sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} (P^n)_{ij}∑n=0∞​(Pn)ij​).

Thus, the expected number of steps until absorption is given by the vector F1TF \mathbf{1}^TF1T, where 1\mathbf{1}1 is a column vector of ones.

Note

Given an irreducible Markov chain, to compute the expected number of steps needed to go from state iii to the first visit to state jjj, one can change the chain into one where state jjj is absorbing, and compute the expected number of steps until absorption when starting at state iii.

Definition: Galton-Watson Branching Process

A (Galton-Watson) branching process is a discrete Markov chain Z0,Z1,…,Zn,…Z_0, Z_1, \ldots, Z_n, \ldotsZ0​,Z1​,…,Zn​,… where,

  • The state space is the non-negative integers.

  • Z0=1Z_0 = 1Z0​=1 (i.e., the process starts with one individual).

  • ZnZ_nZn​ is the sum X1+X2+…+XZn−1X_1 + X_2 + \ldots + X_{Z_{n - 1}}X1​+X2​+…+XZn−1​​, where XiX_iXi​ are independent random non-negative integers all with the same offspring distribution,

Zn=∑i=1Zn−1XiZ_n = \sum_{i = 1}^{Z_{n - 1}} X_iZn​=i=1∑Zn−1​​Xi​

Connecting each of the ZnZ_nZn​ individuals in generation nnn with their offspring in generation n+1n + 1n+1 we get a tree illustrating the branching process.

The offspring distribution is described by the probability vector a=(a0,a1,…)a = (a_0, a_1, \ldots)a=(a0​,a1​,…) where aj=P(Xi=j)a_j = P(X_i = j)aj​=P(Xi​=j).

We will mainly focus on the interesting cases where we assume a0>0a_0 > 0a0​>0 (i.e., there is a positive probability of having no offspring) and a0+a1<1a_0 + a_1 < 1a0​+a1​<1 (i.e., there is a positive probability of having more than one offspring).

Definition: Expected Size of Generations in a Branching Process and Classification of Branching Processes
Note

Note that the state 0 is abosribing, this absorbtion is called extinction. Also, as a0>0a_0 > 0a0​>0, all nonzero states are transient. Let μ=E[Xi]=∑j=0∞j⋅aj\mu = \mathbb{E}[X_i] = \sum_{j=0}^{\infty} j \cdot a_jμ=E[Xi​]=∑j=0∞​j⋅aj​, i.e., the expected number of offspring per individual.

Then, we may compute that,

E[Zn]=E(∑i=1Zn−1)Xi=E(E(∑i=1Zn−1Xi∣Zn−1))=…=E[Zn−1]⋅μ\begin{align*} \mathbb{E}[Z_n] & = \mathbb{E}\left(\sum_{i = 1}^{Z_{n-1}}\right) X_i \newline & = \mathbb{E}\left(\mathbb{E}\left(\sum_{i = 1}^{Z_{n-1}} X_i \mid Z_{n - 1} \right)\right) & = \ldots & = \mathbb{E}[Z_{n - 1}] \cdot \mu \newline \end{align*}E[Zn​]​=E(i=1∑Zn−1​​)Xi​=E(E(i=1∑Zn−1​​Xi​∣Zn−1​))​=…​=E[Zn−1​]⋅μ​

Thus, we directly get that,

E[Zn]=μn⋅E[Z0]⏟≔1=μn\mathbb{E}[Z_n] = \mu^n \cdot \underbrace{\mathbb{E}[Z_0]}_{\coloneqq 1} = \mu^nE[Zn​]=μn⋅:=1E[Z0​]​​=μn

Based on the value of μ\muμ, we classify branching processes into three categories:

  1. Subcritical branching processes: μ<1\mu < 1μ<1. The expected generation size decreases over time, lim⁡n→∞E[Zn]=0\lim_{n \to \infty} \mathbb{E}[Z_n] = 0limn→∞​E[Zn​]=0.

  2. Critical branching processes: μ=1\mu = 1μ=1. The expected generation size remains constant over time, lim⁡n→∞E[Zn]=1\lim_{n \to \infty} \mathbb{E}[Z_n] = 1limn→∞​E[Zn​]=1.

  3. Supercritical branching processes: μ>1\mu > 1μ>1. The expected generation size increases over time, lim⁡n→∞E[Zn]=∞\lim_{n \to \infty} \mathbb{E}[Z_n] = \inftylimn→∞​E[Zn​]=∞.

Note

One can prove that if lim⁡n→∞Zn=0\lim_{n \to \infty} Z_n = 0limn→∞​Zn​=0, then the probability of extinction is 1. We will do this later.

Definition: Variance of Generation Sizes in a Branching Process

Let μ\muμ be defined as above, and let σ2=Var(Xi)\sigma^2 = \mathrm{Var}(X_i)σ2=Var(Xi​) be the variance of the number of children.

Using the law of total variance we get,

Var(Zn)=Var(E[Zn∣Zn−1])+E[Var(Zn∣Zn−1)]=Var(E(∑i=1Zn−1Xi∣Zn−1))+E[Var(∑i=1Zn−1Xi∣Zn−1)]=Var(μZn−1)+E[σ2Zn−1]=μ2Var(Zn−1)+σ2E[Zn−1]\begin{align*} \mathrm{Var}(Z_n) & = \mathrm{Var}(\mathbb{E}[Z_n \mid Z_{n - 1}]) + \mathbb{E}[\mathrm{Var}(Z_n \mid Z_{n - 1})] \newline & = \mathrm{Var}\left(\mathbb{E}\left(\sum_{i = 1}^{Z_{n - 1}} X_i \mid Z_{n - 1}\right)\right) + \mathbb{E}\left[\mathrm{Var}\left(\sum_{i = 1}^{Z_{n - 1}} X_i \mid Z_{n - 1}\right)\right] \newline & = \mathrm{Var}(\mu Z_{n - 1}) + \mathbb{E}[\sigma^2 Z_{n - 1}] \newline & = \mu^2 \mathrm{Var}(Z_{n - 1}) + \sigma^2 \mathbb{E}[Z_{n - 1}] \newline \end{align*}Var(Zn​)​=Var(E[Zn​∣Zn−1​])+E[Var(Zn​∣Zn−1​)]=Var(E(i=1∑Zn−1​​Xi​∣Zn−1​))+E[Var(i=1∑Zn−1​​Xi​∣Zn−1​)]=Var(μZn−1​)+E[σ2Zn−1​]=μ2Var(Zn−1​)+σ2E[Zn−1​]​

From this, we can prove by induction that, for n≥1n \geq 1n≥1,

Var(Zn)=σ2μn−1∑k=0n−1μk={nσ2if μ=1σ2μn−1μn−1μ−1if μ≠1\mathrm{Var}(Z_n) = \sigma^2 \mu^{n - 1} \sum_{k = 0}^{n - 1} \mu^k = \begin{cases} n \sigma^2 & \text{if } \mu = 1 \newline \sigma^2 \mu^{n - 1} \frac{\mu^n - 1}{\mu - 1} & \text{if } \mu \neq 1 \end{cases}Var(Zn​)=σ2μn−1k=0∑n−1​μk={nσ2σ2μn−1μ−1μn−1​​if μ=1if μ=1​
Theorem: Extinction Probability Theorem

Let GXG_XGX​ be the probability generating function for the offspring distribution for a branching process. The probability of eventual extinction is the smallest positive root of the equation GX(s)=sG_X(s) = sGX​(s)=s.

Intuition: Probability Generating Functions

For any discrete random variable XXX taking values in {0,1,2,…}\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}{0,1,2,…}, define the probability function GX(s)G_X(s)GX​(s) as,

GX(s)=E[sX]=∑k=0∞P(X=k)skG_X(s) = \mathbb{E}[s^X] = \sum_{k = 0}^{\infty} P(X = k) s^kGX​(s)=E[sX]=k=0∑∞​P(X=k)sk

The series converges absolutely for ∥s∥≤1\Vert s \Vert \leq 1∥s∥≤1. We assume sss is a real number in [0,1][0, 1][0,1].

We get a 1-1 correspondence between probability vectors on {0,1,2,…}\{0, 1, 2, \ldots\}{0,1,2,…} and functions represented by a series where the coefficients sum to 1.

Specifically, if GX(s)=GY(s)G_X(s) = G_Y(s)GX​(s)=GY​(s) for all sss for random variables XXX and YYY, then XXX and YYY have the same distribution.

Note: Properties of Probability Generating Functions

  • To go from XXX to GX(s)G_X(s)GX​(s), we compute the (in)finite sum.

  • To go from GX(s)G_X(s)GX​(s) to XXX, we use that we have,

P(X=k)=GX(k)(0)k!P(X = k) = \frac{G_X^{(k)}(0)}{k!}P(X=k)=k!GX(k)​(0)​
  • If XXX and YYY are independent random variables, then,
GX+Y(s)=E[sX+Y]=E[sXsY]=E[sX]E[sY]≕GX(s)GY(s)G_{X + Y}(s) = \mathbb{E}[s^{X + Y}] = \mathbb{E}[s^X s^Y] = \mathbb{E}[s^X] \mathbb{E}[s^Y] \eqqcolon G_X(s) G_Y(s)GX+Y​(s)=E[sX+Y]=E[sXsY]=E[sX]E[sY]=:GX​(s)GY​(s)

  • E[X]=GX′(1)\mathbb{E}[X] = G_X^{\prime}(1)E[X]=GX′​(1)

  • E[X(X−1)]=GX′′(1)\mathbb{E}[X(X - 1)] = G_X^{\prime \prime}(1)E[X(X−1)]=GX′′​(1)

  • As a consequence, Var(X)=GX′′(1)+GX′(1)−(GX′(1))2\mathrm{Var}(X) = G_X^{\prime \prime}(1) + G_X^{\prime}(1) - (G_X^{\prime}(1))^2Var(X)=GX′′​(1)+GX′​(1)−(GX′​(1))2

Further, assume we have a branching process Z0,Z1,…Z_0, Z_1, \ldotsZ0​,Z1​,… with independent offspring variables XiX_iXi​ all with the same distribution as XXX. By writing Gn(s)=GZn(s)=E[sZn]G_n(s) = G_{Z_n}(s) = \mathbb{E}[s^{Z_n}]Gn​(s)=GZn​​(s)=E[sZn​] and GX(s)=E[sX]G_X(s) = \mathbb{E}[s^X]GX​(s)=E[sX], we get,

Gn(s)=E[s∑k=1Zn−1Xk]=E[E[s∑k=1Zn−1Xk∣Zn−1]]=E[E[∏k=1Zn−1sXk∣Zn−1]]=E[G(s)Zn−1]=Gn−1(GX(s))\begin{align*} G_n(s) & = \mathbb{E}[s^{\sum_{k = 1}^{Z_{n - 1}} X_k}] \newline & = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[s^{\sum_{k = 1}^{Z_{n - 1}} X_k} \mid Z_{n - 1}]] \newline & = \mathbb{E}\left[\mathbb{E}\left[\prod_{k = 1}^{Z_{n - 1}} s^{X_k} \mid Z_{n - 1}\right]\right] \newline & = \mathbb{E}[G(s)^{Z_{n - 1}}] \newline & = G_{n - 1}(G_X(s)) \newline \end{align*}Gn​(s)​=E[s∑k=1Zn−1​​Xk​]=E[E[s∑k=1Zn−1​​Xk​∣Zn−1​]]=E[E[k=1∏Zn−1​​sXk​∣Zn−1​]]=E[G(s)Zn−1​]=Gn−1​(GX​(s))​

As G0(s)=E[sZ0]=sG_0(s) = \mathbb{E}[s^{Z_0}] = sG0​(s)=E[sZ0​]=s, it follows that,

Gn(s)=GX(GX(…GX(s)…))⏟n timesG_n(s) = \underbrace{G_X(G_X(\ldots G_X(s) \ldots))}_{n \text{ times}}Gn​(s)=n timesGX​(GX​(…GX​(s)…))​​
Theorem: Extinction Probability Theorem (Addition)

Let GXG_XGX​ be the probability generating function for the offspring distribution for a branching process. The probability of eventual extinction is the smallest positive root of the equation GX(s)=sG_X(s) = sGX​(s)=s. Also, if the process is critical (μ=1\mu = 1μ=1) then the extinction probability is 1.

Proof: Of the last sentence

In the critical case,

GX′(1)=E[X]=μ=1G_X^{\prime}(1) = \mathbb{E}[X] = \mu = 1GX′​(1)=E[X]=μ=1

As GX′′(s)>0G_X^{\prime \prime}(s) > 0GX′′​(s)>0 for s∈(0,1)s \in (0, 1)s∈(0,1), we get that GX′(s)<1G_X^{\prime}(s) < 1GX′​(s)<1 for s∈(0,1)s \in (0, 1)s∈(0,1), and dds(G(s)−s)<0\frac{d}{ds}(G(s) - s) < 0dsd​(G(s)−s)<0 for s∈(0,1)s \in (0, 1)s∈(0,1). As G(1)−1=0G(1) - 1 = 0G(1)−1=0 for any probability generating function, we get that G(s)=sG(s) = sG(s)=s has its smallest positive root at s=1s = 1s=1.

Theorem: Strong Law of Large Numbers

Strong law of large numbers of samples: If Y1,Y2,…,YmY_1, Y_2, \ldots, Y_mY1​,Y2​,…,Ym​ and YYY are i.i.d random variables from a distribution with finite mean, and if E[r(Y)]\mathbb{E}[r(Y)]E[r(Y)] exists, then, with probability 1,

lim⁡m→∞r(Y1)+r(Y2)+…+r(Ym)m=E[r(Y)]\lim_{m \to \infty} \frac{r(Y_1) + r(Y_2) + \ldots + r(Y_m)}{m} = \mathbb{E}[r(Y)]m→∞lim​mr(Y1​)+r(Y2​)+…+r(Ym​)​=E[r(Y)]
Theorem: Strong Law of Large Numbers for Markov Chains

Strong law of large numbers for Markov chains: If X0,X1,…X_0, X_1, \ldotsX0​,X1​,… is an ergodic Markov chain with stationary distribution π\piπ, and if EX∼π[r(X)]\mathbb{E}_{X \sim \pi}[r(X)]EX∼π​[r(X)] exists, then, with probability 1,

lim⁡m→∞r(X0)+r(X1)+…+r(Xm)m=EX∼π[r(X)]\lim_{m \to \infty} \frac{r(X_0) + r(X_1) + \ldots + r(X_m)}{m} = \mathbb{E}_{X \sim \pi}[r(X)]m→∞lim​mr(X0​)+r(X1​)+…+r(Xm​)​=EX∼π​[r(X)]

where XXX has distribution π\piπ.

Note

When using this theorem in practice, one might improve accuracy by throwing away the first sequence X1,…,XsX_1, \ldots, X_sX1​,…,Xs​ for s<ms < ms<m before computing the average. The first sequence is then called the burn-in.

Definition: The Metropolis-Hastings Algorithm

The Metropolis-Hastings algorithm is a method to construct a Markov chain having a desired limiting distribution. Let θ\thetaθ be a random variable with probability mass function π(θ)\pi( \theta)π(θ). We also assume given a proposal distribution T(θnew∣θ)T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta)T(θnew​∣θ), which, for every given θ\thetaθ, provides a PMF for a new θnew\theta_{\text{new}}θnew​.

Finally, we define, for θ\thetaθ and θnew\theta_{\text{new}}θnew​, the acceptance probability,

a=min⁡(1,π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)π(θ)T(θnew∣θ))a = \min\left(1, \frac{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}})}{\pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta)}\right)a=min(1,π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)​)

The Metropolis-Hastings algorithm is: Starting with some initial value θ0\theta_0θ0​, generate θ1,θ2,…\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldotsθ1​,θ2​,… by, at each step, proposing a new θ\thetaθ based on the old using the proposal function and accepting it with probability aaa. If it is not accepted, the old value is used again.

If this defines an ergodic Markov chain, its unique stationary distribution is π(θ)\pi(\theta)π(θ).

Proof

Let PPP be the transition matrix of the Markov chain defined by the Metropolis-Hastings algorithm. We need to show that πP=π\pi P = \piπP=π. It suffices to show that the detailed balance equations are satisfied, i.e., that,

π(θ)Pθθnew=π(θnew)Pθnewθ\pi(\theta) P_{\theta \theta_{\text{new}}} = \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) P_{\theta_{\text{new}} \theta}π(θ)Pθθnew​​=π(θnew​)Pθnew​θ​

for all states θ\thetaθ and θnew\theta_{\text{new}}θnew​. We have,

Pθθnew=T(θnew∣θ)⋅a=T(θnew∣θ)⋅min⁡(1,π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)π(θ)T(θnew∣θ))P_{\theta \theta_{\text{new}}} = T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \cdot a = T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \cdot \min\left(1, \frac{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}})}{\pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta)}\right)Pθθnew​​=T(θnew​∣θ)⋅a=T(θnew​∣θ)⋅min(1,π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)​)

and,

Pθnewθ=T(θ∣θnew)⋅a′=T(θ∣θnew)⋅min⁡(1,π(θ)T(θnew∣θ)π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew))P_{\theta_{\text{new}} \theta} = T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}}) \cdot a^{\prime} = T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}}) \cdot \min\left(1, \frac{\pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta)}{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}})}\right)Pθnew​θ​=T(θ∣θnew​)⋅a′=T(θ∣θnew​)⋅min(1,π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)​)

If π(θ)T(θnew∣θ)≤π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)\pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \leq \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}})π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)≤π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​), then,

π(θ)Pθθnew=π(θ)T(θnew∣θ)⋅1=π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)⋅π(θ)T(θnew∣θ)π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)=π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)⋅a′=π(θnew)Pθnewθ\begin{align*} \pi(\theta) P_{\theta \theta_{\text{new}}} & = \pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \cdot 1 \newline & = \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}}) \cdot \frac{\pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta)}{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}})} \newline & = \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}}) \cdot a^{\prime} \newline & = \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) P_{\theta_{\text{new}} \theta} \end{align*}π(θ)Pθθnew​​​=π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)⋅1=π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)⋅π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)​=π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)⋅a′=π(θnew​)Pθnew​θ​​

If π(θ)T(θnew∣θ)>π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)\pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) > \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}})π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)>π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​), then,

π(θ)Pθθnew=π(θ)T(θnew∣θ)⋅π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)π(θ)T(θnew∣θ)=π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)⋅1=π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)⋅a′=π(θnew)Pθnewθ\begin{align*} \pi(\theta) P_{\theta \theta_{\text{new}}} & = \pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) \cdot \frac{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}})}{\pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta)} \newline & = \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}}) \cdot 1 \newline & = \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}}) \cdot a^{\prime} \newline & = \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) P_{\theta_{\text{new}} \theta} \end{align*}π(θ)Pθθnew​​​=π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)⋅π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)​=π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)⋅1=π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)⋅a′=π(θnew​)Pθnew​θ​​

Thus, in both cases, the detailed balance equations are satisfied, so π\piπ is the stationary distribution of the chain.

Definition: Gibbs Sampling

For any probability model over a vector θ=(θ1,θ2,…,θn)\theta = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n)θ=(θ1​,θ2​,…,θn​), consider a Metropolis-Hastings proposal function changing only one coordinate, with the value of this coordinate simulated from the conditional distribution given the remaining coordinates. The acceptance probability is always 1, so the proposal is always accepted.

Proof

Let θ=(θ1,θ2,…,θn)\theta = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n)θ=(θ1​,θ2​,…,θn​) and θnew=(θ1,…,θi−1,θi,new,θi+1,…,θn)\theta_{\text{new}} = (\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, \theta_{i,\text{new}}, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)θnew​=(θ1​,…,θi−1​,θi,new​,θi+1​,…,θn​) differ only in coordinate iii. The proposal distribution is,

T(θnew∣θ)=P(θi=θi,new∣θ1,…,θi−1,θi+1,…,θn)T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) = P(\theta_i = \theta_{i,\text{new}} \mid \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)T(θnew​∣θ)=P(θi​=θi,new​∣θ1​,…,θi−1​,θi+1​,…,θn​)

and,

T(θ∣θnew)=P(θi=θi∣θ1,…,θi−1,θi+1,…,θn)T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}}) = P(\theta_i = \theta_i \mid \theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)T(θ∣θnew​)=P(θi​=θi​∣θ1​,…,θi−1​,θi+1​,…,θn​)

Using the definition of conditional probability, we have,

T(θnew∣θ)=P(θ1,…,θi−1,θi,new,θi+1,…,θn)P(θ1,…,θi−1,θi+1,…,θn)=π(θnew)∑xπ(θ1,…,θi−1,x,θi+1,…,θn),\begin{align*} T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta) & = \frac{P(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, \theta_{i,\text{new}}, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)} {P(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)} \\ & = \frac{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}})} {\sum_x \pi(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, x, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)} , \end{align*}T(θnew​∣θ)​=P(θ1​,…,θi−1​,θi+1​,…,θn​)P(θ1​,…,θi−1​,θi,new​,θi+1​,…,θn​)​=∑x​π(θ1​,…,θi−1​,x,θi+1​,…,θn​)π(θnew​)​,​

and,

T(θ∣θnew)=P(θ1,…,θi−1,θi,θi+1,…,θn)P(θ1,…,θi−1,θi+1,…,θn)=π(θ)∑xπ(θ1,…,θi−1,x,θi+1,…,θn),\begin{align*} T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}}) & = \frac{P(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, \theta_i, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)} {P(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)} \\ & = \frac{\pi(\theta)} {\sum_x \pi(\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_{i - 1}, x, \theta_{i + 1}, \ldots, \theta_n)} , \end{align*}T(θ∣θnew​)​=P(θ1​,…,θi−1​,θi+1​,…,θn​)P(θ1​,…,θi−1​,θi​,θi+1​,…,θn​)​=∑x​π(θ1​,…,θi−1​,x,θi+1​,…,θn​)π(θ)​,​

Thus, we have,

π(θnew)T(θ∣θnew)π(θ)T(θnew∣θ)=π(θnew)π(θ)∑xπ(θ1,…,θi−1,x,θi+1,…,θn)π(θ)π(θnew)∑xπ(θ1,…,θi−1,x,θi+1,…,θn)=π(θnew)π(θ)π(θ)π(θnew)=1,\begin{align*} \frac{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) T(\theta \mid \theta_{\text{new}})} {\pi(\theta) T(\theta_{\text{new}} \mid \theta)} & = \frac{ \pi(\theta_{\text{new}}) \frac{\pi(\theta)}{\sum_x \pi(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_{i-1},x,\theta_{i+1},\ldots,\theta_n)} }{ \pi(\theta) \frac{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}})}{\sum_x \pi(\theta_1,\ldots,\theta_{i-1},x,\theta_{i+1},\ldots,\theta_n)} } \\ & = \frac{\pi(\theta_{\text{new}})\pi(\theta)} {\pi(\theta)\pi(\theta_{\text{new}})} = 1 , \end{align*}π(θ)T(θnew​∣θ)π(θnew​)T(θ∣θnew​)​​=π(θ)∑x​π(θ1​,…,θi−1​,x,θi+1​,…,θn​)π(θnew​)​π(θnew​)∑x​π(θ1​,…,θi−1​,x,θi+1​,…,θn​)π(θ)​​=π(θ)π(θnew​)π(θnew​)π(θ)​=1,​

Thus, the proposal is always accepted.

Putting together an algorithm updating different coordinates in different steps may create an ergodic Markov chain.

This is called Gibbs sampling. Sometimes the conditional distributions are easy to derive. Then this is an easy-to-use version of Metropolis-Hastings.

Definition: The Ising Model

Uses a grid of vertices; We will assume an n×nn \times nn×n grid. Two vertices vvv and www are neighbours, denoted v∼wv \sim wv∼w, if they are next to each other in the grid.

Each vertex vvv can have value +1+1+1 or −1-1−1 (called its “spin”); We denote this by σv=1\sigma_{v} = 1σv​=1 or σv=−1\sigma_{v} = -1σv​=−1.

A configuration σ\sigmaσ consists of a choice of +1+1+1 or −1-1−1 for each vertex. Thus, the set Ω\OmegaΩ of possible configurations has 2(n2)2^{(n^2)}2(n2) elements. We define the energy of a configuration as E(σ)=−∑v∼wσvσwE(\sigma) = - \sum_{v \sim w} \sigma_v \sigma_wE(σ)=−∑v∼w​σv​σw​.

The Gibbs distribution is the probability mass function on Ω\OmegaΩ defined by,

π(σ)∝exp⁡(−βE(σ))\pi(\sigma) \propto \exp(-\beta E(\sigma))π(σ)∝exp(−βE(σ))

where β\betaβ is a parameter of the model; 1β\frac{1}{\beta}β1​ is called the temperature.

It turns out that when the temperature is high, samples from the model will show a chaotic pattern of spins, but when the temperature sinks below the phase transition value, samples will show chunks of neighbouring vertices with the same spin, i.e., the system will be “magnetized”.

Intuition: Simulating from the Ising Model using Gibbs Sampling

For a vertex configuration σ\sigmaσ and a vertex vvv, let σ−v\sigma_{-v}σ−v​ denote the part of σ\sigmaσ that does not involve vvv. Prpose a new configuration σ⋆\sigma^{\star}σ⋆ given an old configuration σ\sigmaσ by first choosing a vertex vvv, then, let σ⋆\sigma^{\star}σ⋆ be identical to σ\sigmaσ except possibly at vvv. Decide the spin at vvv using the conditional distribution given σ−v\sigma_{-v}σ−v​,

π(σ⋆∣σ−v)=π(σv=1,σ−v)π(σ−v)=π(σv=1,σ−v)π(σv=1,σ−v)+π(σv=−1,σ−v)=11+π(σv=−1,σ−v)π(σv=1,σ−v)=11+exp⁡(−βE(σv=−1,σ−v)+βE(σv=1,σ−v))=11+exp⁡(β∑v∼wσvσw∣σv=−1−β∑v∼wσvσw∣σv=1)=11+exp⁡(−2β∑v∼wσw)\begin{align*} \pi(\sigma^{\star} \mid \sigma_{-v}) & = \frac{\pi(\sigma_v = 1, \sigma_{-v})}{\pi(\sigma_{-v})} \newline & = \frac{\pi(\sigma_v = 1, \sigma_{-v})}{\pi(\sigma_v = 1, \sigma_{-v}) + \pi(\sigma_v = -1, \sigma_{-v})} \newline & = \frac{1}{1 + \frac{\pi(\sigma_v = -1, \sigma_{-v})}{\pi(\sigma_v = 1, \sigma_{-v})}} \newline & = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-\beta E(\sigma_v = -1, \sigma_{-v}) + \beta E(\sigma_v = 1, \sigma_{-v}))} \newline & = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(\beta \sum_{v \sim w} \sigma_v \sigma_w \mid \sigma_v = -1 - \beta \sum_{v \sim w} \sigma_v \sigma_w \mid \sigma_v = 1)} \newline & = \frac{1}{1 + \exp(-2 \beta \sum_{v \sim w} \sigma_w)} \newline \end{align*}π(σ⋆∣σ−v​)​=π(σ−v​)π(σv​=1,σ−v​)​=π(σv​=1,σ−v​)+π(σv​=−1,σ−v​)π(σv​=1,σ−v​)​=1+π(σv​=1,σ−v​)π(σv​=−1,σ−v​)​1​=1+exp(−βE(σv​=−1,σ−v​)+βE(σv​=1,σ−v​))1​=1+exp(β∑v∼w​σv​σw​∣σv​=−1−β∑v∼w​σv​σw​∣σv​=1)1​=1+exp(−2β∑v∼w​σw​)1​​
Intuition: Perfect Sampling

Given an ergodic Markov chain with finite state space of size kkk and limiting distribution π\piπ. The idea is that, given nnn prove that XnX_nXn​ actually has reached the limit distribution π\piπ.

If we can prove that the distribution at XnX_nXn​ is independent of the starting state X0X_0X0​, then we might be able to conclude that XnX_nXn​ has the limiting distribution π\piπ.

Construct kkk Markov chains that are dependent (“coupled”) but which are marginally Markov chains as above. If they start at the kkk possible value at X0X_0X0​ but have identical values at XnX_nXn​, then we are done.

Note
Intuition: Using same source of randomness for all chains

Consider the chains X−n(j),…,X0(j)X^{(j)}_{-n}, \ldots, X^{(j)}_0X−n(j)​,…,X0(j)​ for j=1,2,…,kj = 1, 2, \ldots, kj=1,2,…,k. Instead of simulating Xi+1(j)X^{(j)}_{i + 1}Xi+1(j)​ based on Xi(j)X^{(j)}_iXi(j)​ independently for each j, we define a function ggg so that Xi+1(j)=g(Xi(j),Ui)X^{(j)}_{i + 1} = g(X^{(j)}_i, U_i)Xi+1(j)​=g(Xi(j)​,Ui​) for all jjj where Ui∼Uniform(0,1)U_i \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0, 1)Ui​∼Uniform(0,1).

Thus, if two chains have identical values in XiX_iXi​, they will also be identical in Xi+1X_{i + 1}Xi+1​.

Thus, for a particular nnn, if all chains have not converged at X0X_0X0​, we simulate kkk chains from X−2nX_{-2n}X−2n​ to X−nX_{-n}X−n​. They might only hit a subset of the kkk states at X−nX_{-n}X−n​ and thus might coalesce to one state at X0X_0X0​ using the old simulations. If not, double nnn again.

Definition: Counting Process

A counting process {Nt}t∈I\{N_t\}_{t \in \mathcal{I}}{Nt​}t∈I​ is a stochastic process where I=R0+\mathcal{I} = \mathbb{R}_0^+I=R0+​, where the state space is the non-negative integers, and where 0≤s≤t0 \leq s \leq t0≤s≤t implies Ns≤NtN_s \leq N_tNs​≤Nt​.

Generally, when s<t,Nt−Nss < t, N_t - N_ss<t,Nt​−Ns​ counts the number of “events” (in the loose meaning “things happening”) in (s,t](s, t](s,t].

A realization of NtN_tNt​ is then a function of ttt that is a right-continuous step function.

Definition: Poisson Process I

A Poisson process {Nt}t≥0\{N_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Nt​}t≥0​ with parameter λ>0\lambda > 0λ>0 is a counting process, fulfilling,

  1. N0=0N_0 = 0N0​=0.
  2. Nt∼Poisson(λt)N_t \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda t)Nt​∼Poisson(λt) for all t>0t > 0t>0.
  3. Stationary Increments: Nt+s−NsN_{t + s} - N_sNt+s​−Ns​ has the same distribution as NtN_tNt​ for all s>0,t>0s > 0, t > 0s>0,t>0.
  4. Independent Increments: Nt−NsN_t - N_sNt​−Ns​ and Nr−NqN_r - N_qNr​−Nq​ are independent, when 0≤q<r≤s<t0 \leq q < r \leq s < t0≤q<r≤s<t.
Note

It is not obvious that such a process necessarily exists. Further, E[Nt]=λt\mathbb{E}[N_t] = \lambda tE[Nt​]=λt, thus what one is counting occurs with a rate of λ\lambdaλ items per time unit.

Definition: Poisson Process II

Let X1,X2,…X_1, X_2, \ldotsX1​,X2​,… be a sequence of i.i.d. exponential random variables with parameter λ\lambdaλ. Let N0=0N_0 = 0N0​=0 and, for t>0t > 0t>0,

Nt≔max⁡{n:X1+…+Xn≤t}.N_t \coloneqq \max\{n : X_1 + \ldots + X_n \leq t\}.Nt​:=max{n:X1​+…+Xn​≤t}.

Then, {Nt}t≥0\{N_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Nt​}t≥0​ is a Poisson process with parameter λ\lambdaλ.

Proof

Firstly, if we start with a Poisson process (definition I), and let X1,X2,…X_1, X_2, \ldotsX1​,X2​,… be the inter-arrival times, i.e., the time between event 1 and event 2, event 2 and event 3, etc., then these are i.i.d. exponential random variables with parameter λ\lambdaλ. Thus, NtN_tNt​ will be defined as above.

Conversely, if we construct NtN_tNt​ as above, all properties of definition I can be verified.

Let {Mt}t≥0\{M_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Mt​}t≥0​ be defined as Mt≔Nt+s−NsM_t \coloneqq N_{t + s} - N_sMt​:=Nt+s​−Ns​ for some s>0s > 0s>0.

  • It is a counting process by definition.
  • N0=0N_0 = 0N0​=0 by definition.
  • Stationary increments follow from the memoryless property of the exponential distribution: Mk=Ns+t+k−Ns+t=max⁡{n:XNs+t+1+…+XNs+t+n≤k}M_k = N_{s + t + k} - N_{s + t} = \max\{n : X_{N_{s + t} + 1} + \ldots + X_{N_{s + t} + n} \leq k\}Mk​=Ns+t+k​−Ns+t​=max{n:XNs+t​+1​+…+XNs+t​+n​≤k}, which has the same distribution as NkN_kNk​.
  • Independent increments follow from the independence of the XiX_iXi​‘s.
  • Mk=Mk+s−Ns=max⁡{n:XNs+1+…+XNs+n≤k}M_k = M_{k + s} - N_s = \max\{n : X_{N_s + 1} + \ldots + X_{N_s + n} \leq k\}Mk​=Mk+s​−Ns​=max{n:XNs​+1​+…+XNs​+n​≤k}, which has a Poisson(λk)\mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda k)Poisson(λk) distribution.

We call Sn≔X1+…+XnS_n \coloneqq X_1 + \ldots + X_nSn​:=X1​+…+Xn​ the arrival times of the process. Let M=min⁡{X1,…,Xn}M = \min\{X_1, \ldots, X_n\}M=min{X1​,…,Xn​}, where, independently for each iii, Xi∼Exponential(λi)X_i \sim \mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda_i)Xi​∼Exponential(λi​), then,

M∼Exponential(λ1+…+λn),P(M=Xk)=λkλ1+…+λn.M \sim \mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda_1 + \ldots + \lambda_n), \quad P(M = X_k) = \frac{\lambda_k}{\lambda_1 + \ldots + \lambda_n}.M∼Exponential(λ1​+…+λn​),P(M=Xk​)=λ1​+…+λn​λk​​.
Proof

Since the XiX_iXi​‘s are independent,

P(X1>t)P(X2>t)…P(Xn>t)=e−λ1te−λ2t…e−λnt=e−(λ1+…+λn)t\begin{align*} P(X_1 > t) P(X_2 > t) \ldots P(X_n > t) & = e^{-\lambda_1 t} e^{-\lambda_2 t} \ldots e^{-\lambda_n t} \newline & = e^{-(\lambda_1 + \ldots + \lambda_n) t} \newline \end{align*}P(X1​>t)P(X2​>t)…P(Xn​>t)​=e−λ1​te−λ2​t…e−λn​t=e−(λ1​+…+λn​)t​

Thus, M∼Exponential(λ1+…+λn)M \sim \mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda_1 + \ldots + \lambda_n)M∼Exponential(λ1​+…+λn​).

Further,

P(M=X1)=P(X2≥X1,…,Xn≥X1)=E[I[X2≥X1,…,Xn≥X1]]=E[E[I[X2≥X1,…,Xn≥X1]∣X1]]=E[P(X2≥X1,…,Xn≥X1∣X1)]=E[e−λ2X1…e−λnX1∣X1]=E[e−(λ2+…+λn)X1]=∫0∞e−(λ2+…+λn)xλ1e−λ1x dx=λ1∫0∞e−(λ1+λ2+…+λn)x dx=λ1λ1+λ2+…+λn[−1λ1+λ2+…+λne−(λ1+λ2+…+λn)x]0∞⏟=1=λ1λ1+λ2+…+λn ■\begin{align*} P(M = X_1) & = P(X_2 \geq X_1, \ldots, X_n \geq X_1) \newline & = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{I}[X_2 \geq X_1, \ldots, X_n \geq X_1]] \newline & = \mathbb{E}[\mathbb{E}[\mathbb{I}[X_2 \geq X_1, \ldots, X_n \geq X_1] \mid X_1]] \newline & = \mathbb{E}[P(X_2 \geq X_1, \ldots, X_n \geq X_1 \mid X_1)] \newline & = \mathbb{E}[e^{-\lambda_2 X_1} \ldots e^{-\lambda_n X_1} \mid X_1] \newline & = \mathbb{E}[e^{-(\lambda_2 + \ldots + \lambda_n) X_1}] \newline & = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(\lambda_2 + \ldots + \lambda_n) x} \lambda_1 e^{-\lambda_1 x} \ dx \newline & = \lambda_1 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \ldots + \lambda_n) x} \ dx \newline & = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \ldots + \lambda_n} \underbrace{\left[-\frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \ldots + \lambda_n} e^{-(\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \ldots + \lambda_n) x}\right]_{0}^{\infty}}_{= 1} \newline & = \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \ldots + \lambda_n} \ _\blacksquare \end{align*}P(M=X1​)​=P(X2​≥X1​,…,Xn​≥X1​)=E[I[X2​≥X1​,…,Xn​≥X1​]]=E[E[I[X2​≥X1​,…,Xn​≥X1​]∣X1​]]=E[P(X2​≥X1​,…,Xn​≥X1​∣X1​)]=E[e−λ2​X1​…e−λn​X1​∣X1​]=E[e−(λ2​+…+λn​)X1​]=∫0∞​e−(λ2​+…+λn​)xλ1​e−λ1​x dx=λ1​∫0∞​e−(λ1​+λ2​+…+λn​)x dx=λ1​+λ2​+…+λn​λ1​​=1[−λ1​+λ2​+…+λn​1​e−(λ1​+λ2​+…+λn​)x]0∞​​​=λ1​+λ2​+…+λn​λ1​​ ■​​
Definition: Poisson Process III

A Poisson process {Nt}t≥0\{N_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Nt​}t≥0​ with parameter λ>0\lambda > 0λ>0 is a counting process, fulfilling,

  • N0=0N_0 = 0N0​=0.
  • The process has stationary and independent increments.
P(Nh=0)=1−λh+o(h)P(Nh=1)=λh+o(h)P(Nh>1)=o(h)\begin{align*} P(N_h = 0) & = 1 - \lambda h + o(h) \newline P(N_h = 1) & = \lambda h + o(h) \newline P(N_h > 1) & = o(h) \newline \end{align*}P(Nh​=0)P(Nh​=1)P(Nh​>1)​=1−λh+o(h)=λh+o(h)=o(h)​

for all h>0h > 0h>0, where o(h)o(h)o(h) is a function such that lim⁡h→0o(h)h=0\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{o(h)}{h} = 0limh→0​ho(h)​=0.

Lemma: Superposition of Poisson Processes

Let {Nt(1)}t≥0,…,{Nt(n)}t≥0\{N_t^{(1)}\}_{t \geq 0}, \ldots, \{N_t^{(n)}\}_{t \geq 0}{Nt(1)​}t≥0​,…,{Nt(n)​}t≥0​ be independent Poisson processes with parameters λp1,…,λpn\lambda p_1, \ldots, \lambda p_nλp1​,…,λpn​, respectively, where p=(p1,…,pn)p = (p_1, \ldots, p_n)p=(p1​,…,pn​) is a probability vector. If c=(c1,…,cn)c = (c_1, \ldots, c_n)c=(c1​,…,cn​) are the counts after time ttt (i.e., ci=Nt(i)c_i = N_t^{(i)}ci​=Nt(i)​), then, the conditional distribution of (Nt(1),…,Nt(n))(N_t^{(1)}, \ldots, N_t^{(n)})(Nt(1)​,…,Nt(n)​), an equivalent model is,

c∼Multinomial(Nt,p),c \sim \mathrm{Multinomial}(N_t, p),c∼Multinomial(Nt​,p),

where {Nt}t≥0\{N_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Nt​}t≥0​ is a Poisson process with parameter λ\lambdaλ.

Proof

Using the model with independent Poisson processes, the probability of observing the count vector ccc after time ttt is (denoting N=c1+…+cnN = c_1 + \ldots + c_nN=c1​+…+cn​),

∏i=1nPoisson(ci;λpit)=∏i=1ne−λpit(λpit)cici!=e−λt(λt)N∏i=1npicici!=e−λt(λt)NN!⋅N!c1!c2!…cn!p1c1p2c2…pncn=Poisson(N;λt)⋅Multinomial(c;N,p)\begin{align*} \prod_{i = 1}^n \mathrm{Poisson}(c_i; \lambda p_i t) & = \prod_{i = 1}^n e^{-\lambda p_i t} \frac{(\lambda p_i t)^{c_i}}{c_i!} \newline & = e^{-\lambda t} (\lambda t)^N \prod_{i = 1}^n \frac{p_i^{c_i}}{c_i!} \newline & = e^{-\lambda t} \frac{(\lambda t)^N}{N!} \cdot \frac{N!}{c_1! c_2! \ldots c_n!} p_1^{c_1} p_2^{c_2} \ldots p_n^{c_n} \newline & = \mathrm{Poisson}(N; \lambda t) \cdot \mathrm{Multinomial}(c; N, p) \newline \end{align*}i=1∏n​Poisson(ci​;λpi​t)​=i=1∏n​e−λpi​tci​!(λpi​t)ci​​=e−λt(λt)Ni=1∏n​ci​!pici​​​=e−λtN!(λt)N​⋅c1​!c2​!…cn​!N!​p1c1​​p2c2​​…pncn​​=Poisson(N;λt)⋅Multinomial(c;N,p)​

The process for NNN inherits independent and stationary increments from the sub-processes, so it follows it also a Poisson process with parameter λ\lambdaλ.

Lemma: Uniformly Distributed Arrival Times

Let {Nt}t≥0\{N_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Nt​}t≥0​ be a Poisson process with parameter λ\lambdaλ. If we fix that Nt=kN_t = kNt​=k, and we select uniformly randomly one of the kkk arrivals, then its arrival time is uniformly distributed on the interval [0,t][0, t][0,t].

Proof

Let S1,S2,…,SkS_1, S_2, \ldots, S_kS1​,S2​,…,Sk​ be the arrival times given that Nt=kN_t = kNt​=k.

P(Sk≥s∣k uniformly random in 1,…,n,Nt=n)=1n∑k=1nP(Sk≥s∣Nt=n)=1n∑k=1n∑j=0k−1P(Ns=j∣Nt=n)=1n∑k=1n∑j=0k−1P(Ns=j)P(Nt−s=n−j)P(Nt=n)=1n∑k=1n∑j=0k−1e−λs(λs)jj!e−λ(t−s)(λ(t−s))n−j(n−j)!e−λt(λt)nn!=1n∑j=0n−1(n−j)n!j!(n−j)!(st)j(1−st)n−j=[∑j=0n−1n!j!(n−j−1)!(st)j(1−st)n−j−1]⋅(1−st)=1−st\begin{align*} P(S_k \geq s \mid k \text{ uniformly random in } {1, \ldots, n}, N_t = n) & = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k = 1}^{n} P(S_k \geq s \mid N_t = n) \newline & = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k = 1}^{n} \sum_{j = 0}^{k - 1} P(N_s = j \mid N_t = n) \newline & = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k = 1}^{n} \sum_{j = 0}^{k - 1} \frac{P(N_s = j) P(N_{t - s} = n - j)}{P(N_t = n)} \newline & = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k = 1}^{n} \sum_{j = 0}^{k - 1} \frac{e^{-\lambda s} \frac{(\lambda s)^j}{j!} e^{-\lambda (t - s)} \frac{(\lambda (t - s))^{n - j}}{(n - j)!}}{e^{-\lambda t} \frac{(\lambda t)^n}{n!}} \newline & = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j = 0}^{n - 1} (n - j) \frac{n!}{j! (n - j)!} \left(\frac{s}{t}\right)^j \left(1 - \frac{s}{t}\right)^{n - j} \newline & = \left[\sum_{j = 0}^{n - 1} \frac{n!}{j! (n - j - 1)!} \left(\frac{s}{t}\right)^j \left(1 - \frac{s}{t}\right)^{n - j - 1}\right] \cdot \left(1 - \frac{s}{t}\right) \newline & = 1 - \frac{s}{t} \end{align*}P(Sk​≥s∣k uniformly random in 1,…,n,Nt​=n)​=n1​k=1∑n​P(Sk​≥s∣Nt​=n)=n1​k=1∑n​j=0∑k−1​P(Ns​=j∣Nt​=n)=n1​k=1∑n​j=0∑k−1​P(Nt​=n)P(Ns​=j)P(Nt−s​=n−j)​=n1​k=1∑n​j=0∑k−1​e−λtn!(λt)n​e−λsj!(λs)j​e−λ(t−s)(n−j)!(λ(t−s))n−j​​=n1​j=0∑n−1​(n−j)j!(n−j)!n!​(ts​)j(1−ts​)n−j=[j=0∑n−1​j!(n−j−1)!n!​(ts​)j(1−ts​)n−j−1]⋅(1−ts​)=1−ts​​
Definition: Spatial Poisson Process

A collection of random variables {NA}A⊆Rd\{N_A\}_{A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d}{NA​}A⊆Rd​ is a spatial Poisson process with parameter λ\lambdaλ if,

  • For each bounded set A⊆RdA \subseteq \mathbb{R}^dA⊆Rd, NAN_ANA​ has a Poisson distribution with parameter λ∣A∣\lambda |A|λ∣A∣.
  • Whenever A⊆B,NA≤NBA \subseteq B, N_A \leq N_BA⊆B,NA​≤NB​ (i.e., spatial counting process).
  • Whenever AAA and BBB are disjoint, NAN_ANA​ and NBN_BNB​ are independent.
Definition: Non-Homogeneous Poisson Process

A counting process {Nt}t≥0\{N_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Nt​}t≥0​ is a non-homogeneous Poisson process with intensity function λ(t)\lambda(t)λ(t) if,

  • N0=0N_0 = 0N0​=0.
  • For 0<s<t0 < s < t0<s<t,
Nt−Ns∼Poisson(∫stλ(x) dx).N_t - N_s \sim \mathrm{Poisson}\left(\int_{s}^{t} \lambda(x) \ dx\right).Nt​−Ns​∼Poisson(∫st​λ(x) dx).
  • It has independent increments.
Definition: Continuous-Time Markov Chain

A continuous-time stochastic process {Xt}t≥0\{X_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Xt​}t≥0​ with discrete state space S\mathcal{S}S is a continuous-time Markov chain if,

P(Xt+s=j∣Xs=i,Xu,0≤u<s)=P(Xt+s=j∣Xs=i),P(X_{t + s} = j \mid X_s = i, X_u, 0 \leq u < s) = P(X_{t + s} = j \mid X_s = i),P(Xt+s​=j∣Xs​=i,Xu​,0≤u<s)=P(Xt+s​=j∣Xs​=i),

where s,t≥0s, t \geq 0s,t≥0 and i,j,Xu∈Si, j, X_u \in \mathcal{S}i,j,Xu​∈S.

The process is time-homogeneous if for s,t≥0s, t \geq 0s,t≥0 and all i,j∈Si, j \in \mathcal{S}i,j∈S,

P(Xt+s=j∣Xs=i)=P(Xt=j∣X0=i).P(X_{t + s} = j \mid X_s = i) = P(X_t = j \mid X_0 = i).P(Xt+s​=j∣Xs​=i)=P(Xt​=j∣X0​=i).

We then define the transition function as the matrix function P(t)P(t)P(t) with entries of the matrix given by,

P(t)ij=P(Xt=j∣X0=i).P(t)_{ij} = P(X_t = j \mid X_0 = i).P(t)ij​=P(Xt​=j∣X0​=i).
Theorem: Chapman-Kolmogorov Equations

For the transition function P(t)P(t)P(t) we have,

  • P(s+t)=P(s)P(t)P(s + t) = P(s) P(t)P(s+t)=P(s)P(t)
  • P(0)=IP(0) = IP(0)=I (identity matrix)
Proof

For the first property; If they are same for an arbitrary element i,ji, ji,j of the matrix, then they are equal.

P(s+t)ij=P(Xs+t=j∣Xs=i)=∑kP(Xs+t=j,Xs=k∣X0=i)=∑kP(Xs=k∣X0=i)P(Xs+t=j∣Xs=k,X0=i)=∑kP(Xs=k∣X0=i)P(Xs+t=j∣Xs=k)=∑kP(Xs=k∣X0=i)P(Xt=j∣X0=k)=∑kP(s)ikP(t)kj=(P(s)P(t))ij ■\begin{align*} P(s + t)_{ij} & = P(X_{s + t} = j \mid X_s = i) \newline & = \sum_k P(X_{s + t} = j, X_s = k \mid X_0 = i) \newline & = \sum_k P(X_s = k \mid X_0 = i) P(X_{s + t} = j \mid X_s = k, X_0 = i) \newline & = \sum_k P(X_s = k \mid X_0 = i) P(X_{s + t} = j \mid X_s = k) \newline & = \sum_k P(X_s = k \mid X_0 = i) P(X_t = j \mid X_0 = k) \newline & = \sum_k P(s)_{ik} P(t)_{kj} \newline & = (P(s) P(t))_{ij} \ _\blacksquare \end{align*}P(s+t)ij​​=P(Xs+t​=j∣Xs​=i)=k∑​P(Xs+t​=j,Xs​=k∣X0​=i)=k∑​P(Xs​=k∣X0​=i)P(Xs+t​=j∣Xs​=k,X0​=i)=k∑​P(Xs​=k∣X0​=i)P(Xs+t​=j∣Xs​=k)=k∑​P(Xs​=k∣X0​=i)P(Xt​=j∣X0​=k)=k∑​P(s)ik​P(t)kj​=(P(s)P(t))ij​ ■​​

The second property follows directly from the definition of the transition function.

Intuition: Holding Times

Define TiT_iTi​ as the time the continuous-time Markov chain {Xt}t≥0\{X_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Xt​}t≥0​ that always starts in state iii stays in iii before moving to a different state, so that for any s>0s > 0s>0,

P(Ti>s)=P(Xu=i,0≤u≤s)P(T_i > s) = P(X_u = i, 0 \leq u \leq s)P(Ti​>s)=P(Xu​=i,0≤u≤s)

The distribution of TiT_iTi​ is memoryless and thus exponential. We define qiq_iqi​ such that,

Ti∼Exponential(qi)T_i \sim \mathrm{Exponential}(q_i)Ti​∼Exponential(qi​)

Remember that this means that the average time the process stays in iii is 1qi\frac{1}{q_i}qi​1​. The rate of transition out of the state is qiq_iqi​.

Note

We can have qi=0q_i = 0qi​=0, meaning that the state iii is absorbing,

P(Ti>s)=1.P(T_i > s) = 1.P(Ti​>s)=1.
Intuition: Embedded Markov Chain

We can define a new stochastic process by listing the states the chain visits. This will be a discrete time Markov chain called the embedded Markov chain with transition matrix P~\tilde{P}P~.

Note

The transition matrix P~\tilde{P}P~ has zeros along its diagonal! Why? Because the process cannot stay in the same state when it makes a transition.

Further, note that the continuous-time Markov chain is completely determined by the expected holding times (1q1,…,1qk)(\frac{1}{q_1}, \ldots, \frac{1}{q_k})(q1​1​,…,qk​1​) and the transition matrix of the embedded Markov chain P~\tilde{P}P~.

Intuition: Constructing a Continuous-Time Markov Chain

A way to describe a continuous-time Markov chain is to describe k×(k−1)k \times (k - 1)k×(k−1) independent “alarm clocks”.

For states iii and jjj so that i≠ji \neq ji=j, let qijq_{ij}qij​ be the parameter of an Exponentially distributed random variable representing the time until an “alarm clock” rings.

When in state iii, wait until the first alarm clock qiq_iqi​ rings, then move to the state given by the index jjj of that alarm clock. This defines a continuous-time Markov chain.

The time until the first alarm clock qiq_iqi​ rings is Exponentially distributed with parameter given by,

qi=qi1+qi2+…+qi,i−1+qi,i+1+…+qik.q_i = q_{i1} + q_{i2} + \ldots + q_{i, i - 1} + q_{i, i + 1} + \ldots + q_{ik}.qi​=qi1​+qi2​+…+qi,i−1​+qi,i+1​+…+qik​.

i.e., the parameter of the holding time distribution at iii.

The chain is completely described by the rates qijq_{ij}qij​.

Further, we saw also that the chain is completely described by the pijp_{ij}pij​ and the qiq_iqi​. The relationship is described by the equation above, and,

pij=qijqi1+qi2+…+qi,i−1+qi,i+1+…+qik=qijqi.p_{ij} = \frac{q_{ij}}{q_{i1} + q_{i2} + \ldots + q_{i, i - 1} + q_{i, i + 1} + \ldots + q_{ik}} = \frac{q_{ij}}{q_i}.pij​=qi1​+qi2​+…+qi,i−1​+qi,i+1​+…+qik​qij​​=qi​qij​​.

for i≠ji \neq ji=j.

Intuition: Infinitesimal Generator Matrix

To relate P(t)P(t)P(t) to the qijq_{ij}qij​‘s, we first relate them to P′(0)P^{\prime}(0)P′(0).

Assuming P(t)P(t)P(t) is differentiable, we can show that,

P′(0)=[−q1q12q13…q1kq21−q2q23…q2k⋮⋮⋮⋱⋮qk1qk2qk3…−qk]=Q,P^{\prime}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} -q_1 & q_{12} & q_{13} & \ldots & q_{1k} \newline q_{21} & -q_2 & q_{23} & \ldots & q_{2k} \newline \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \newline q_{k1} & q_{k2} & q_{k3} & \ldots & -q_k \newline \end{bmatrix} = Q,P′(0)=​−q1​q21​⋮qk1​​q12​−q2​⋮qk2​​q13​q23​⋮qk3​​……⋱…​q1k​q2k​⋮−qk​​​=Q,
Proof: Lazy “proof”

Firstly, let’s show that Q12=P′(0)12=q12Q_{12} = P^{\prime}(0)_{12} = q_{12}Q12​=P′(0)12​=q12​.

Q12=lim⁡h→0P(Xh=2∣X0=1)h=lim⁡h→0P(h)12h=lim⁡h→0P(h)12−P(0)12h≕P′(0)12\begin{align*} Q_{12} & = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(X_h = 2 \mid X_0 = 1)}{h} \newline & = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(h)_{12}}{h} \newline & = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(h)_{12} - P(0)_{12}}{h} \newline & \eqqcolon P^{\prime}(0)_{12} \newline \end{align*}Q12​​=h→0lim​hP(Xh​=2∣X0​=1)​=h→0lim​hP(h)12​​=h→0lim​hP(h)12​−P(0)12​​=:P′(0)12​​

Secondly, show that −Q1=P′(0)11-Q_1 = P^{\prime}(0)_{11}−Q1​=P′(0)11​.

−Q1=−[Q12+Q13+…+Q1k]=−lim⁡h→0P(h)12+P(h)13+…+P(h)1kh=−lim⁡h→01−P(h)11h=lim⁡h→0P(h)11−1h=lim⁡h→0P(h)11−P(0)11h≕P′(0)11\begin{align*} -Q_1 & = -[Q_{12} + Q_{13} + \ldots + Q_{1k}] \newline & = - \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(h)_{12} + P(h)_{13} + \ldots + P(h)_{1k}}{h} \newline & = - \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{1 - P(h)_{11}}{h} \newline & = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(h)_{11} - 1}{h} \newline & = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(h)_{11} - P(0)_{11}}{h} \newline & \eqqcolon P^{\prime}(0)_{11} \newline \end{align*}−Q1​​=−[Q12​+Q13​+…+Q1k​]=−h→0lim​hP(h)12​+P(h)13​+…+P(h)1k​​=−h→0lim​h1−P(h)11​​=h→0lim​hP(h)11​−1​=h→0lim​hP(h)11​−P(0)11​​=:P′(0)11​​

The other elements can be shown similarly ■_\blacksquare■​.

Note that the rows of P′(0)P^{\prime}(0)P′(0), i.e., QQQ, sum to zero!

Theorem: Kolmogorov Forward and Backward Equations

The Kolmogorov Forward and Backward equations describe how the transition function P(t)P(t)P(t) evolves over time.

We get that for all t≥0t \geq 0t≥0,

P′(t)=P(t)Q=QP(t).P^{\prime}(t) = P(t) Q = Q P(t).P′(t)=P(t)Q=QP(t).

Thus, what this means in terms of the components of P(t)P(t)P(t),

P′(t)ij=−Pij(t)qj+∑k≠jPik(t)qkjP′(t)ij=−qiPij(t)+∑k≠iqikPkj(t)\begin{align*} P^{\prime}(t)_{ij} & = -P_{ij}(t) q_j + \sum_{k \neq j} P_{ik}(t) q_{kj} \newline P^{\prime}(t)_{ij} & = -q_i P_{ij}(t) + \sum_{k \neq i} q_{ik} P_{kj}(t) \newline \end{align*}P′(t)ij​P′(t)ij​​=−Pij​(t)qj​+k=j∑​Pik​(t)qkj​=−qi​Pij​(t)+k=i∑​qik​Pkj​(t)​

Thus, the above equations define a set of differential equations which the components of the matrix function P(t)P(t)P(t) must satisfy.

Proof
P′(t)=lim⁡h→0P(t+h)−P(t)h=lim⁡h→0P(t)P(h)−P(t)h=lim⁡h→0P(t)P(h)−Ih=P(t)lim⁡h→0P(h)−Ih=P(t)lim⁡h→0P(h)−P(0)h⏟P′(0)=Q=P(t)Q ■\begin{align*} P^{\prime}(t) & = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(t + h) - P(t)}{h} \newline & = \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(t) P(h) - P(t)}{h} \newline & = \lim_{h \to 0} P(t) \frac{P(h) - I}{h} \newline & = P(t) \lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(h) - I}{h} \newline & = P(t) \underbrace{\lim_{h \to 0} \frac{P(h) - P(0)}{h}}_{P^{\prime}(0) = Q} \newline & = P(t) Q \ _\blacksquare \end{align*}P′(t)​=h→0lim​hP(t+h)−P(t)​=h→0lim​hP(t)P(h)−P(t)​=h→0lim​P(t)hP(h)−I​=P(t)h→0lim​hP(h)−I​=P(t)P′(0)=Qh→0lim​hP(h)−P(0)​​​=P(t)Q ■​​

Since we arbitrarily chose to write P(t+h)P(t + h)P(t+h) as P(t)P(h)P(t) P(h)P(t)P(h), we could have equally written it as P(h)P(t)P(h) P(t)P(h)P(t), leading to the other equation, thus completing the proof.

Intuition: Matrix Exponential Solution

The solution to the Kolmogorov Forward and Backward equations is given by the matrix exponential. For any square matrix AAA we can define the matrix exponential as,

eA≔∑n=0∞1n!An=I+A+12A2+16A3+…e^A \coloneqq \sum_{n = 0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n!} A^n = I + A + \frac{1}{2} A^2 + \frac{1}{6} A^3 + \ldotseA:=n=0∑∞​n!1​An=I+A+21​A2+61​A3+…

The series converges for all square matrices AAA (but we will not prove this here).

Some important properties of the matrix exponential are:

  • e0=Ie^0 = Ie0=I
  • eAe−A=Ie^A e^{-A} = IeAe−A=I
  • e(s+t)A=esAetAe^{(s + t) A} = e^{sA} e^{tA}e(s+t)A=esAetA
  • If AB=BAAB = BAAB=BA, then eA+B=eAeB=eBeAe^{A + B} = e^A e^B = e^B e^AeA+B=eAeB=eBeA
  • ∂∂tetA=AetA=etAA\frac{\partial}{\partial t} e^{tA} = A e^{tA} = e^{tA} A∂t∂​etA=AetA=etAA

Note that P(t)=etQP(t) = e^{tQ}P(t)=etQ is the unique solution to the differential equations given by the Kolmogorov Forward and Backward equations with initial condition P(0)=IP(0) = IP(0)=I.

Further, note that P′(t)=QP(t)P^\prime(t) = Q P(t)P′(t)=QP(t) for all t≥0t \geq 0t≥0 and P(0)=IP(0) = IP(0)=I.

In R one can use the expm package to compute matrix exponentials.

Definition: Multinomial Distribution

A vector x=(x1,x2,…,xk)x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)x=(x1​,x2​,…,xk​) of non-negative integers has a Multinomial distribution with parameters nnn and ppp, where n>0n > 0n>0 is an integer and ppp is a probability vector of length kkk, if ∑i=1kxi=n\sum_{i = 1}^k x_i = n∑i=1k​xi​=n and the probability mass function is given by,

π(x∣n,p)=n!x1!x2!…xk!p1x1p2x2…pkxk.\pi(x \mid n, p) = \frac{n!}{x_1! x_2! \ldots x_k!} p_1^{x_1} p_2^{x_2} \ldots p_k^{x_k}.π(x∣n,p)=x1​!x2​!…xk​!n!​p1x1​​p2x2​​…pkxk​​.

We write x∼Multinomial(n,p)x \sim \mathrm{Multinomial}(n, p)x∼Multinomial(n,p).

Definition: Dirichlet Distribution

A vector p=(p1,p2,…,pk)p = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k)p=(p1​,p2​,…,pk​) of non-negative real numbers satisfying ∑i=1kpi=1\sum_{i = 1}^k p_i = 1∑i=1k​pi​=1 has a Dirichlet distribution with parameter vector α=(α1,α2,…,αk)\alpha = (\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_k)α=(α1​,α2​,…,αk​), if it has probability density function,

π(p∣α)=Γ(α1+α2+…+αk)Γ(α1)Γ(α2)…Γ(αk)p1α1−1p2α2−1…pkαk−1,\pi(p \mid \alpha) = \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \ldots + \alpha_k)}{\Gamma(\alpha_1) \Gamma(\alpha_2) \ldots \Gamma(\alpha_k)} p_1^{\alpha_1 - 1} p_2^{\alpha_2 - 1} \ldots p_k^{\alpha_k - 1},π(p∣α)=Γ(α1​)Γ(α2​)…Γ(αk​)Γ(α1​+α2​+…+αk​)​p1α1​−1​p2α2​−1​…pkαk​−1​,

where αi>0\alpha_i > 0αi​>0 for all i=1,2,…,ki = 1, 2, \ldots, ki=1,2,…,k. We write p∼Dirichlet(α)p \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha)p∼Dirichlet(α).

Theorem: Multinomial-Dirichlet Conjugacy

Let x=(x1,x2,…,xk)x = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_k)x=(x1​,x2​,…,xk​) be a vector of counts with x∼Multinomial(n,p)x \sim \mathrm{Multinomial}(n, p)x∼Multinomial(n,p) and prior p∼Dirichlet(α)p \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha)p∼Dirichlet(α). Then the posterior distribution of ppp given xxx is,

p∣x∼Dirichlet(α1+x1,α2+x2,…,αk+xk).p \mid x \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha_1 + x_1, \alpha_2 + x_2, \ldots, \alpha_k + x_k).p∣x∼Dirichlet(α1​+x1​,α2​+x2​,…,αk​+xk​).

Further, if p∼Dirichlet(α)p \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha)p∼Dirichlet(α), then E[pi]=αi∑j=1kαj\mathbb{E}[p_i] = \frac{\alpha_i}{\sum_{j = 1}^k \alpha_j}E[pi​]=∑j=1k​αj​αi​​ for i=1,2,…,ki = 1, 2, \ldots, ki=1,2,…,k.

Proof: Multinomial-Dirichlet Conjugacy

Let π(x∣p)=Multinomial(x;n,p)\pi(x \mid p) = \mathrm{Multinomial}(x; n, p)π(x∣p)=Multinomial(x;n,p) and π(p)=Dirichlet(p;α)\pi(p) = \mathrm{Dirichlet}(p; \alpha)π(p)=Dirichlet(p;α), respectively. Then,

π(p∣x)∝π(x∣p)π(p)=Multinomial(x;n,p)Dirichlet(p;α)∝pp1x1p2x2…pkxk⋅p1α1−1p2α2−1…pkαk−1∝pp1α1+x1−1p2α2+x2−1…pkαk+xk−1=Dirichlet(p;α1+x1,α2+x2,…,αk+xk) ■\begin{align*} \pi(p \mid x) & \propto \pi(x \mid p) \pi(p) \newline & = \mathrm{Multinomial}(x; n, p) \mathrm{Dirichlet}(p; \alpha) \newline & \propto_p p_1^{x_1} p_2^{x_2} \ldots p_k^{x_k} \cdot p_1^{\alpha_1 - 1} p_2^{\alpha_2 - 1} \ldots p_k^{\alpha_k - 1} \newline & \propto_p p_1^{\alpha_1 + x_1 - 1} p_2^{\alpha_2 + x_2 - 1} \ldots p_k^{\alpha_k + x_k - 1} \newline & = \mathrm{Dirichlet}(p; \alpha_1 + x_1, \alpha_2 + x_2, \ldots, \alpha_k + x_k) \ _\blacksquare \end{align*}π(p∣x)​∝π(x∣p)π(p)=Multinomial(x;n,p)Dirichlet(p;α)∝p​p1x1​​p2x2​​…pkxk​​⋅p1α1​−1​p2α2​−1​…pkαk​−1​∝p​p1α1​+x1​−1​p2α2​+x2​−1​…pkαk​+xk​−1​=Dirichlet(p;α1​+x1​,α2​+x2​,…,αk​+xk​) ■​​
Proof: Expectation of Dirichlet Distribution

Let p∼Dirichlet(α)p \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha)p∼Dirichlet(α). Then,

E[pi]=∫⋯∫piΓ(α1+α2+…+αk)Γ(α1)Γ(α2)…Γ(αk)p1α1−1p2α2−1…pkαk−1 dp1dp2…dpk=Γ(α1+α2+…+αk)Γ(α1)Γ(α2)…Γ(αk)∫⋯∫p1α1−1p2α2−1…piαi…pkαk−1 dp1dp2…dpk=Γ(α1+α2+…+αk)Γ(α1)Γ(α2)…Γ(αk)⋅Γ(α1)Γ(α2)…Γ(αi+1)…Γ(αk)Γ(α1+α2+…+αk+1)=Γ(αi+1)Γ(αi)⋅Γ(α1+α2+…+αk)Γ(α1+α2+…+αk+1)=αi⋅1α1+α2+…+αk=αi∑j=1kαj ■\begin{align*} \mathbb{E}[p_i] & = \int \cdots \int p_i \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \ldots + \alpha_k)}{\Gamma(\alpha_1) \Gamma(\alpha_2) \ldots \Gamma(\alpha_k)} p_1^{\alpha_1 - 1} p_2^{\alpha_2 - 1} \ldots p_k^{\alpha_k - 1} \ dp_1 dp_2 \ldots dp_k \newline & = \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \ldots + \alpha_k)}{\Gamma(\alpha_1) \Gamma(\alpha_2) \ldots \Gamma(\alpha_k)} \int \cdots \int p_1^{\alpha_1 - 1} p_2^{\alpha_2 - 1} \ldots p_i^{\alpha_i} \ldots p_k^{\alpha_k - 1} \ dp_1 dp_2 \ldots dp_k \newline & = \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \ldots + \alpha_k)}{\Gamma(\alpha_1) \Gamma(\alpha_2) \ldots \Gamma(\alpha_k)} \cdot \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_1) \Gamma(\alpha_2) \ldots \Gamma(\alpha_i + 1) \ldots \Gamma(\alpha_k)}{\Gamma(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \ldots + \alpha_k + 1)} \newline & = \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_i + 1)}{\Gamma(\alpha_i)} \cdot \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \ldots + \alpha_k)}{\Gamma(\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \ldots + \alpha_k + 1)} \newline & = \alpha_i \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \ldots + \alpha_k} \newline & = \frac{\alpha_i}{\sum_{j = 1}^k \alpha_j} \ _\blacksquare \end{align*}E[pi​]​=∫⋯∫pi​Γ(α1​)Γ(α2​)…Γ(αk​)Γ(α1​+α2​+…+αk​)​p1α1​−1​p2α2​−1​…pkαk​−1​ dp1​dp2​…dpk​=Γ(α1​)Γ(α2​)…Γ(αk​)Γ(α1​+α2​+…+αk​)​∫⋯∫p1α1​−1​p2α2​−1​…piαi​​…pkαk​−1​ dp1​dp2​…dpk​=Γ(α1​)Γ(α2​)…Γ(αk​)Γ(α1​+α2​+…+αk​)​⋅Γ(α1​+α2​+…+αk​+1)Γ(α1​)Γ(α2​)…Γ(αi​+1)…Γ(αk​)​=Γ(αi​)Γ(αi​+1)​⋅Γ(α1​+α2​+…+αk​+1)Γ(α1​+α2​+…+αk​)​=αi​⋅α1​+α2​+…+αk​1​=∑j=1k​αj​αi​​ ■​​
Intuition: Predictions for The Multinomial-Dirichlet Model

If p∼Dirichlet(α)p \sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha)p∼Dirichlet(α) and x∣p∼Multinomial(n,p)x \mid p \sim \mathrm{Multinomial}(n, p)x∣p∼Multinomial(n,p), then the predictive distribution is given by,

π(x)=n!x1!x2!…xk!⋅Γ(α1+x1)Γ(α1)⋅Γ(α2+x2)Γ(α2)⋯Γ(αk+xk)Γ(αk)⋅Γ(∑i=1kαi)Γ(n+∑i=1kαi).\pi(x) = \frac{n!}{x_1! x_2! \ldots x_k!} \cdot \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_1 + x_1)}{\Gamma(\alpha_1)} \cdot \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_2 + x_2)}{\Gamma(\alpha_2)} \cdots \frac{\Gamma(\alpha_k + x_k)}{\Gamma(\alpha_k)} \cdot \frac{\Gamma(\sum_{i = 1}^k \alpha_i)}{\Gamma(n + \sum_{i = 1}^k \alpha_i)}.π(x)=x1​!x2​!…xk​!n!​⋅Γ(α1​)Γ(α1​+x1​)​⋅Γ(α2​)Γ(α2​+x2​)​⋯Γ(αk​)Γ(αk​+xk​)​⋅Γ(n+∑i=1k​αi​)Γ(∑i=1k​αi​)​.

For example, if eie_iei​ is the vector with 1 at position iii and zeros elsewhere, then π(x=ei)=αi∑j=1kαj\pi(x = e_i) = \frac{\alpha_i}{\sum_{j = 1}^k \alpha_j}π(x=ei​)=∑j=1k​αj​αi​​. Further, if xnewx_{\text{new}}xnew​ is a vector of new counts, then, as p∣x∼Multinomial(x+α)p \mid x \sim \mathrm{Multinomial}(x + \alpha)p∣x∼Multinomial(x+α), we get,

π(xnew=ei∣x)=αi+xi∑j=1kαj+n.\pi(x_{\text{new}} = e_i \mid x) = \frac{\alpha_i + x_i}{\sum_{j = 1}^k \alpha_j + n}.π(xnew​=ei​∣x)=∑j=1k​αj​+nαi​+xi​​.

The αi\alpha_iαi​ in the prior are often called pseudocounts, as they can be interpreted as prior counts added to the observed counts.

Definition: Hidden Markov Model

A Hidden Markov Model (HMMs) consists of:

  • A Markov chain X0,…,Xn,…X_0, \ldots, X_n, \ldotsX0​,…,Xn​,… and,
  • another sequence Y0,…,Yn,…Y_0, \ldots, Y_n, \ldotsY0​,…,Yn​,… such that,
P(Yk∣Y0,…,Yk−1,X0,…,Xn)=P(Yk∣Xk).P(Y_k \mid Y_0, \ldots, Y_{k - 1}, X_0, \ldots, X_n) = P(Y_k \mid X_k).P(Yk​∣Y0​,…,Yk−1​,X0​,…,Xn​)=P(Yk​∣Xk​).

In some models we may instead have,

P(Yk∣Y0,…,Yk−1,X0,…,Xn)=P(Yk∣Yk−1,Xk).P(Y_k \mid Y_0, \ldots, Y_{k - 1}, X_0, \ldots, X_n) = P(Y_k \mid Y_{k - 1}, X_k).P(Yk​∣Y0​,…,Yk−1​,X0​,…,Xn​)=P(Yk​∣Yk−1​,Xk​).

Generally, Y0,…,YnY_0, \ldots, Y_nY0​,…,Yn​ are called observations and X0,…,XnX_0, \ldots, X_nX0​,…,Xn​ are called hidden states. The XiX_iXi​‘s represent the “underlying process” that the observed values YiY_iYi​‘s depend on. Further, generally the XkX_kXk​ have a finite state space.

Intuition: Inference in HMMs

When the parameters of the HMM are known, we want to know about the values of the hidden variables XiX_iXi​, for example,

  • What is the most likely sequence X0,X1,…,XnX_0, X_1, \ldots, X_nX0​,X1​,…,Xn​ given the data?
  • What is the probability distribution for a single XiX_iXi​ given the data? However, when the parameters of the HMM are unknown, we need to infer these from some data.

If data with all XiX_iXi​ and YiY_iYi​ known is available, inference for parameters is based on counts of transitions. The inference for the Markov chain is exactly as for the Markov chains we have looked at before. The inference for the emission probabilities, i.e., the parameters of P(Yk∣Xk)P(Y_k \mid X_k)P(Yk​∣Xk​), can be done independently of the inference for the Markov chain.

Intuition: Bayesian Inference for Poisson Processes

For a homogeneous Poisson process, we setup up a prior π(λ)\pi(\lambda)π(λ) for its parameter λ\lambdaλ, and find the posterior given the observations.

The likelihood for observing yyy events in an interval of length ttt is given by,

Poisson(y;λt)=exp⁡(−λt)(λt)yy!∝λexp⁡(−λt)λy.\mathrm{Poisson}(y; \lambda t) = \exp(-\lambda t) \frac{(\lambda t)^y}{y!} \propto_{\lambda} \exp(-\lambda t) \lambda^y.Poisson(y;λt)=exp(−λt)y!(λt)y​∝λ​exp(−λt)λy.

A convenient prior to use is λ∼Gamma(α,β)\lambda \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)λ∼Gamma(α,β).

In this case, the posterior becomes,

λ∣D∼Gamma(α+y,β+t),\lambda \mid \mathcal{D} \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha + y, \beta + t),λ∣D∼Gamma(α+y,β+t),

and the predictive distribution for the number of observations yny_nyn​ in a different interval of length uuu becomes,

π(yn)=Negative−Binomial(yn;α,ββ+u).\pi(y_n) = \mathrm{Negative-Binomial}\left(y_n; \alpha, \frac{\beta}{\beta + u}\right).π(yn​)=Negative−Binomial(yn​;α,β+uβ​).
Intuition: Bayesian Inference for Continuous-Time Markov Chains

Lastyl, recall that a continuous-time Markov chain with finite state space is completely characterized by its holding times parameter vector qqq and the transition matrix P~\tilde{P}P~ of its embedded Markov chain.

Parametrizing instead with the “alarm clock” parameters qijq_{ij}qij​ gives an equivalent description of the process.

The two parts of the data can be considered independently are,

  • We learn about P~\tilde{P}P~ from the counts of transitions between states, and
  • We learn about qqq from the observed lengths of stays the process has in each state.

For P~\tilde{P}P~ the situation is analogous to the one for discrete-time Markov chains, except that the diagonal of P~\tilde{P}P~ must be zero, so the prior must exclude the possibility of non-zero diagonal elements.

For example, for P~1\tilde{P}_1P~1​, the first row of P~\tilde{P}P~, we might use the prior Dirichlet(0,1,…,1)\mathrm{Dirichlet}(0, 1, \ldots, 1)Dirichlet(0,1,…,1), i.e., a Dirichlet prior with a zero pseudocount for the first element.

The holding times in state iii are distributed as Exponential(qi)\mathrm{Exponential}(q_i)Exponential(qi​). If we have observed a total holding time of hhh over nnn intervals, that data has likelihood proportional to e−hqiqine^{-hq_i}q_i^ne−hqi​qin​. Using qi∼Gamma(α,β)q_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)qi​∼Gamma(α,β) as prior results in the posterior qi∣D∼Gamma(α+n,β+h)q_i \mid \mathcal{D} \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha + n, \beta + h)qi​∣D∼Gamma(α+n,β+h).

Definition: Stationary Distribution for Continuous-Time Markov Chains

In the context of continuous-time Markov chains, vvv is a stationary distribution if and only if 0=vQ0 = v Q0=vQ, where QQQ is the generator matrix of the chain.

Proof: Stationary Distribution and Generator Matrix

Firstly, let vQ=0vQ = 0vQ=0,

ddt(vP(t))≔vddtP(t)≕vQP(t)=0\begin{align*} \frac{d}{dt} (v P(t)) & \coloneqq v \frac{d}{dt} P(t) \newline & \eqqcolon v Q P(t) \newline & = 0 \newline \end{align*}dtd​(vP(t))​:=vdtd​P(t)=:vQP(t)=0​

as vQ=0vQ = 0vQ=0 by assumption. Thus, vP(t)v P(t)vP(t) is constant in ttt, i.e., vP(t)=vP(0)=vI=vv P(t) = v P(0) = v I = vvP(t)=vP(0)=vI=v for all t≥0t \geq 0t≥0.

Conversely, let vvv be a stationary distribution, i.e., v=vP(t)v = v P(t)v=vP(t) for all t≥0t \geq 0t≥0. Then,

ddt(vP(t))≔vddtP(t)≕vQP(t)=0\begin{align*} \frac{d}{dt} (v P(t)) & \coloneqq v \frac{d}{dt} P(t) \newline & \eqqcolon v Q P(t) \newline & = 0 \newline \end{align*}dtd​(vP(t))​:=vdtd​P(t)=:vQP(t)=0​

as vP(t)v P(t)vP(t) is constant in ttt by assumption. In particular, for t=0t = 0t=0 we get vQP(0)=vQI=vQ=0v Q P(0) = v Q I = v Q = 0vQP(0)=vQI=vQ=0. Thus, the proof is complete ■_\blacksquare■​.

Intuition: Long-Term Behavior of Continuous-Time Markov Chains

A continuous-time Markov chain is irreducible if for all iii and jjj there exists a t>0t > 0t>0 such that Pij(t)>0P_{ij}(t) > 0Pij​(t)>0, i.e., it is possible to get to any state from any state.

However, periodic continuous-time Markov chains do not exist. If Pij(t)>0P_{ij}(t) > 0Pij​(t)>0 for some t>0t > 0t>0, then Pij(t)>0P_{ij}(t) > 0Pij​(t)>0 for all t>0t > 0t>0.

Theorem: Fundamental Limit Theorem for Continuous-Time Markov Chains

Let {Xt}tgeq0\{X_t\}_{t \\geq 0}{Xt​}tgeq0​ be a finite, irreducible, continuous-time Markov chain with transition function P(t)P(t)P(t). Then there exists a unique positive stationary distribution vector vvv which is also the limiting distribution.

The limiting distribution of such a chain can be found as the unique vvv satisfying vQ=0v Q = 0vQ=0

Definition: Absorbing State

An absorbing state is one where the rate of leaving it is zero.

Assume {Xt}tgeq0\{X_t\}_{t \\geq 0}{Xt​}tgeq0​ is a continuous-time Markov chain with kkk states. Assume the last state is absorbing and the rest are not (If the chain is irreducible they are then transient).

WE have that qk=0q_k = 0qk​=0 and the entire last row must consist of zeros. We thus get,

Q=[V⋆00].Q = \begin{bmatrix} V & \star \newline \mathbf{0} & 0 \newline \end{bmatrix}.Q=[V0​⋆0​].

Let FFF be the (k−1)×(k−1)(k - 1) \times (k - 1)(k−1)×(k−1) matrix such that FijF_{ij}Fij​ (with i<k,j<ki < k, j < ki<k,j<k) is the expected time spent in state jjj when the chain starts in iii. We can show that F=−V−1F = -V^{-1}F=−V−1.

Proof: Expected Time in Transient States Before Absorption

Generally, we can define DDD as the matrix with (1q1,…,1qk)(\frac{1}{q_1}, \ldots, \frac{1}{q_k})(q1​1​,…,qk​1​) along its diagonal, with all other entries zero. If there are no absorbing states,

P~=DQ+Ik.\tilde{P} = DQ + I_k.P~=DQ+Ik​.

Write A_A\_A_ for a square matrix without its last row and column, so, e.g., Q=VQ_ = VQ=​V.

If the last state is absorbing, i.e., qk=0q_k = 0qk​=0, we get,

P~_=D_Q_+Ik−1.\tilde{P}\_ = D\_ Q\_ + I_{k - 1}.P~_=D_Q_+Ik−1​.

Further, let F′F^{\prime}F′ be the matrix where Fij′F^{\prime}_{ij}Fij′​ is the expected number of stays in state jjj before absorbtion when starting in state iii. As the lengths of stays and changes in states are independent, we get Fij′=Fij1qjF^{\prime}_{ij} = F_{ij} \frac{1}{q_j}Fij′​=Fij​qj​1​ and thus,

F=F′D.F = F^{\prime} D_.F=F′D.​

By the theory for discrete-time Markov chains with absorbing states, we have,

F′=(Ik−1−P~)−1.F^{\prime} = (I_{k - 1} - \tilde{P}_)^{-1}.F′=(Ik−1​−P~)−1​.

Thus,

F=F′D_=(Ik−1−P~_)−1D_=(Ik−1−(D_Q_+Ik−1))−1D_=(−D_Q_)−1D_=−Q_−1D_−1D_=−Q_−1≕−V−1 ■\begin{align*} F & = F^{\prime} D\_ \newline & = (I_{k - 1} - \tilde{P}\_)^{-1} D\_ \newline & = (I_{k - 1} - (D\_ Q\_ + I_{k - 1}))^{-1} D\_ \newline & = (-D\_ Q\_)^{-1} D\_ \newline & = -Q\_^{-1} D\_^{-1} D\_ \newline & = -Q\_^{-1} \newline & \eqqcolon -V^{-1} \ _\blacksquare \end{align*}F​=F′D_=(Ik−1​−P~_)−1D_=(Ik−1​−(D_Q_+Ik−1​))−1D_=(−D_Q_)−1D_=−Q_−1D_−1D_=−Q_−1=:−V−1 ■​​

FFF is called the fundamental matrix (similar to the discrete-time case).

Note

If the chain starts in state iii, the expected time until absorption is the sum of the iii-th row of FFF. Thus, the expected times until absorption are given by the matrix product F1F1F1 of FFF with a column of 1s.

Intuition: Stationary Distribution of the Embedded Markov Chain

The embedded chain of a continuous-time Markov chain: The discrete-time Markov chain where holding times are ignored.

The stationary distribution for the embedded chain and for the continuous-time chain are generally not the same!

However, there is a simple relationship: A probability vector π\piπ is a stationary distribution for a continuous-time Markov chain if and only if ψ\psiψ is a stationary distribution for the embedded chain, where ψj=Cπjqj\psi_j = C \pi_j q_jψj​=Cπj​qj​ for a constant CCC making the entries sum to 1.

Proof: Stationary Distribution of the Embedded Markov Chain

Using notation as above, we have P~=DQ+I\tilde{P} = DQ + IP~=DQ+I. For any vector vvv, we get,

vP~=v(DQ+I)=vDQ+v.v \tilde{P} = v (DQ + I) = v DQ + v.vP~=v(DQ+I)=vDQ+v.

so vP~=vv \tilde{P} = vvP~=v if and only if vDQ=0v DQ = 0vDQ=0.

Definition: Global Balance Equations

Let v=(v1,v2,…,vn)v = (v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n)v=(v1​,v2​,…,vn​) be the stationary distribution of a continuous-time Markov chain with generator matrix QQQ. At vvv, the flow into a state must be equal to the flow out of the state, by the definition of stationary distribution. Which are exactly the equations we get from vQ=0v Q = 0vQ=0,

(v1,v2,…,vn)[−q1q12⋯q1nq21−q2⋯q2n⋮⋮⋱⋮qn1qn2⋯−qn](v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n) \begin{bmatrix} -q_1 & q_{12} & \cdots & q_{1n} \newline q_{21} & -q_2 & \cdots & q_{2n} \newline \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \newline q_{n1} & q_{n2} & \cdots & -q_n \newline \end{bmatrix}(v1​,v2​,…,vn​)​−q1​q21​⋮qn1​​q12​−q2​⋮qn2​​⋯⋯⋱⋯​q1n​q2n​⋮−qn​​​

Thus, because vQ=0vQ = 0vQ=0, for each state jjj we have,

∑i≠jviqij=vjqj.\sum_{i \neq j} v_i q_{ij} = v_j q_j.i=j∑​vi​qij​=vj​qj​.

This is called the global balance equations.

One can generalize this to: If AAA is a set of states, then the long term rates of movement into and out of AAA are the same,

∑i∈A∑j∉Aviqij=∑i∉A∑j∈Aviqij.\sum_{i \in A} \sum_{j \notin A} v_i q_{ij} = \sum_{i \notin A} \sum_{j \in A} v_i q_{ij}.i∈A∑​j∈/A∑​vi​qij​=i∈/A∑​j∈A∑​vi​qij​.
Definition: Local Balance Equations and Time-Reversible Chains

A stronger condition than global balance is local balance. The flow between every pair of states is balanced.

Formally, an irreducible continuous-time Markov chain with stationary distribution vvv is said to be time-reversible if, for all states iii and jjj,

viqij=vjqji.v_i q_{ij} = v_j q_{ji}.vi​qij​=vj​qji​.
Note

The rate of observed changes from iii to jjj is the same as the rate of observed change from jjj to iii. Thus, this is also called time-reversibility, as the process looks the same when observed backward in time.

Further, If a probability vector vvv satisfies the local balance equations, then it is a stationary distribution of the chain.

Definition: Markov Process on a Tree

Firstly, a tree is a connected (undirected) graph with no cycles. Thus, a transition graph gives rise to an undirected graph by connecting all nodes between which there is some rate of transition. Further, assume that the transition graph of an irreducible continuous-time Markov chain is (or gives rise to) a tree.

In a tree, any edge between two states divides all states into two groups (disjoint sets), each on each side of the edge. Thus, the flow must be balanced between across each edge.

In this scenario, the global balance condition then implies the local balance condition over this edge.

Definition: Birth-and-Death Process

A birth-and-death process is a continuous-time Markov chain where the state space is the set of non-negative integers and transitions only occur to neighboring states. The process is necessarily time-reversible, as the transition graph is a tree (in fact a line, if we further assume irreducibility).

We denote the births from iii to i+1i + 1i+1 with λi\lambda_iλi​, and the rate of deaths from iii to i−1i - 1i−1 with μi\mu_iμi​. Thus, the generator matrix has the form,

Q=[−λ0λ000⋯μ1−(λ1+μ1)λ10⋯0μ2−(λ2+μ2)λ2⋯00μ3−(λ3+μ3)⋯⋮⋮⋮⋮⋱]Q = \begin{bmatrix} -\lambda_0 & \lambda_0 & 0 & 0 & \cdots \newline \mu_1 & -(\lambda_1 + \mu_1) & \lambda_1 & 0 & \cdots \newline 0 & \mu_2 & -(\lambda_2 + \mu_2) & \lambda_2 & \cdots \newline 0 & 0 & \mu_3 & -(\lambda_3 + \mu_3) & \cdots \newline \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots \newline \end{bmatrix}Q=​−λ0​μ1​00⋮​λ0​−(λ1​+μ1​)μ2​0⋮​0λ1​−(λ2​+μ2​)μ3​⋮​00λ2​−(λ3​+μ3​)⋮​⋯⋯⋯⋯⋱​​

where λi,μi>0\lambda_i, \mu_i > 0λi​,μi​>0 for all i≥0i \geq 0i≥0.

Provided that ∑k=1∞∏i=1kλi−1μi<∞\sum_{k = 1}^{\infty} \prod_{i = 1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_{i - 1}}{\mu_{i}} < \infty∑k=1∞​∏i=1k​μi​λi−1​​<∞, (i.e., the stationary distribution exists), the stationary distribution is given by,

vk=v0∏i=1kλi−1μi,v0=(1+∑k=1∞∏i=1kλi−1μi)−1.\begin{align*} v_k & = v_0 \prod_{i = 1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_{i - 1}}{\mu_i}, \newline v_0 & = \left(1 + \sum_{k = 1}^{\infty} \prod_{i = 1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_{i - 1}}{\mu_i}\right)^{-1}. \newline \end{align*}vk​v0​​=v0​i=1∏k​μi​λi−1​​,=(1+k=1∑∞​i=1∏k​μi​λi−1​​)−1.​
Proof: Stationary Distribution of Birth-and-Death Processes

Using the local balance equations, we have,

vkλk=vk+1μk+1,\begin{align*} v_k \lambda_k & = v_{k + 1} \mu_{k + 1}, \newline \end{align*}vk​λk​​=vk+1​μk+1​,​

which gives,

vk+1=vkλkμk+1=v0∏i=1k+1λi−1μi.\begin{align*} v_{k + 1} & = v_k \frac{\lambda_k}{\mu_{k + 1}} \newline & = v_0 \prod_{i = 1}^{k + 1} \frac{\lambda_{i - 1}}{\mu_i}. \newline \end{align*}vk+1​​=vk​μk+1​λk​​=v0​i=1∏k+1​μi​λi−1​​.​

Further, as vvv is a probability vector, we have,

1=∑k=0∞vk=v0(1+∑k=1∞∏i=1kλi−1μi).⇒v0=(1+∑k=1∞∏i=1kλi−1μi)−1.\begin{align*} 1 & = \sum_{k = 0}^{\infty} v_k \newline & = v_0 \left(1 + \sum_{k = 1}^{\infty} \prod_{i = 1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_{i - 1}}{\mu_i}\right). \newline & \Rightarrow v_0 = \left(1 + \sum_{k = 1}^{\infty} \prod_{i = 1}^{k} \frac{\lambda_{i - 1}}{\mu_i}\right)^{-1}. \newline \end{align*}1​=k=0∑∞​vk​=v0​(1+k=1∑∞​i=1∏k​μi​λi−1​​).⇒v0​=(1+k=1∑∞​i=1∏k​μi​λi−1​​)−1.​

Thus, the proof is complete. ■_\blacksquare■​

Definition: Brownian Motion

Brownian motion is a continuous-time stochastic process {Bt}t≥0\{B_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Bt​}t≥0​ with the following properties:

  1. B0=0B_0 = 0B0​=0.

  2. For t>0t >0t>0, Bt∼N(0,t)B_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0, t)Bt​∼N(0,t) (i.e., normally distributed with mean 000 and variance ttt).

  3. For s,t>0s, t > 0s,t>0, Bt+s−Bs∼N(0,t)B_{t + s} - B_s \sim \mathcal{N}(0, t)Bt+s​−Bs​∼N(0,t) (i.e., the increments are stationary).

  4. For 0≤q<r≤s<t0 \leq q < r \leq s < t0≤q<r≤s<t, Bt−BsB_t - B_sBt​−Bs​ is independent of Br−BqB_r - B_qBr​−Bq​ (i.e., the increments are independent).

  5. The function t↦Btt \mapsto B_tt↦Bt​ is continuous with probability 111 (almost surely).

Intuition: Brownian Motion as Limit of Random Walks

A random walk is a discrete-time Markov chain S0,S1,S2,…S_0, S_1, S_2, \ldotsS0​,S1​,S2​,… where S0=0S_0 = 0S0​=0 and,

Sn=Y1+Y2+…+Yn,S_n = Y_1 + Y_2 + \ldots + Y_n,Sn​=Y1​+Y2​+…+Yn​,

and Y1,Y2,…Y_1, Y_2, \ldotsY1​,Y2​,… are i.i.d. random variables. Assume E[Yi]=0\mathbb{E}[Y_i] = 0E[Yi​]=0.

Further, if we assume Var(Yi)=1\mathrm{Var}(Y_i) = 1Var(Yi​)=1, we get Var(Sn)=n\mathrm{Var}(S_n) = nVar(Sn​)=n.

Interpolating between the values SnS_nSn​ we can make this into a continuous-time process StS_tSt​ where Var(St)≈t\mathrm{Var}(S_t) \approx tVar(St​)≈t.

We may scale with an s>0s > 0s>0 to get processes St(s)=SstsS_t^{(s)} = \frac{S_{st}}{\sqrt{s}}St(s)​=s​Sst​​ where we get lim⁡s→∞Var(St(s))=t\lim_{s \to \infty} \mathrm{Var}(S_t^{(s)}) = tlims→∞​Var(St(s)​)=t.

It turns out that the processes St(s)S_t^{(s)}St(s)​ when s→∞s \to \inftys→∞ are exactly Brownian motion, no matter what type of YiY_iYi​ we start with.

This is the Donsker’s invariance principle, which is a generalization of the central limit theorem to stochastic processes 1.

Definition: Gaussian Process

A Gaussian process is a continuous-time stochastic process {Xt}t≥0\{X_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Xt​}t≥0​ with the property that for all n≥1n \geq 1n≥1 and 0≤t1<t2<…<tn0 \leq t_1 < t_2 < \ldots < t_n0≤t1​<t2​<…<tn​, Xt1,Xt2,…,XtnX_{t_1}, X_{t_2}, \ldots, X_{t_n}Xt1​​,Xt2​​,…,Xtn​​ have a multivariate normal distribution.

Thus, a Gaussian process is completely determined by its mean function E[Xt]\mathbb{E}[X_t]E[Xt​] and its covariance function Cov(Xs,Xt)\mathrm{Cov}(X_s, X_t)Cov(Xs​,Xt​).

Intuition: Brownian Motion as a Gaussian Process

Brownian motion is a Gaussian process, as we can show that any a1Bt1+a2Bt2+…+anBtna_1 B_{t_1} + a_2 B_{t_2} + \ldots + a_n B_{t_n}a1​Bt1​​+a2​Bt2​​+…+an​Btn​​ is normally distributed.

A Gaussian process {Xt}t≥0\{X_t\}_{t \geq 0}{Xt​}t≥0​ is a Brownian motion if and only if,

  1. X0=0X_0 = 0X0​=0.

  2. E[Xt]=0\mathbb{E}[X_t] = 0E[Xt​]=0 for all ttt.

  3. Cov(Xs,Xt)=min⁡(s,t)\mathrm{Cov}(X_s, X_t) = \min(s, t)Cov(Xs​,Xt​)=min(s,t) for all s,ts, ts,t.

  4. The function t↦Xtt \mapsto X_tt↦Xt​ is continuous with probability 111 (almost surely).

Intuition: Transformations of Brownian Motion

The following transformations of Brownian motion also yield Brownian motion:

  • {−Bt}t≥0\{-B_t\}_{t \geq 0}{−Bt​}t≥0​, negating the process yields another (reflected) Brownian motion.
